The device's UDID (unique device identifier). The system requires this value if the enrollment type is a device enrollment.
The local mobile user's GUID or the network user's GUID from an Open Directory record.
A string that the client provides in the second `User-Authenticate` request after receiving `DigestChallenge` from the server on the first `User-Authenticate` request.

notes.content:
A `UserAuthenticate` handshake usually consists of two transactions between the client and the server. Upon receiving the first request from the client, the server needs to respond with a `200` status code and a dictionary containing a `DigestChallenge` key (string).

A zero-length `DigestChallenge` from the server indicates that it doesn't require an `AuthToken` for the user. Otherwise, the client generates a digest from the user's short name, the user's clear-text password, and the `DigestChallenge` value that the server provides. The client sends the resulting digest in a second `UserAuthenticate` request to the server, which validates the response and returns a dictionary that contains an `AuthToken` value that the device sends in subsequent commands on the user channel (for both the `ServerURL` and `CheckInURL` endpoints).

If the server rejects the `DigestResponse` value because of an invalid password, it needs to return a `200` response and an empty `AuthToken` value. If the server isn't going to manage the user, it returns a `410` status code to the initial `UserAuthenticate` request. The client doesn't make any additional requests to the server on behalf of the user for the duration of the login session.

The next time the user logs in, the client sends a new request and the server can optionally return `410` again. The `AuthToken` remains valid until the next time the client sends a `UserAuthenticate` request. The client initiates a handshake each time a mobile or network user logs in.

items.title: Account Configuration Command
items.summary: Create and configure a local administrator account on a device.
items.searchKeys: ManagedLocalUserShortName AutoSetupAdminAccounts AutoSetupAdminAccountItem hidden passwordHash fullName shortName LockPrimaryAccountInfo DontAutoPopulatePrimaryAccountInfo PrimaryAccountUserName PrimaryAccountFullName SetPrimarySetupAccountAsRegularUser SkipPrimarySetupAccountCreation
items.searchTitles: Account Configuration Command  hidden passwordHash fullName shortName
items.searchContent:
Create and configure a local administrator account on a device. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. If present, this is the short name of the local account to manage, which can also be the account that results from setting `AutoSetupAdminAccounts` to `true`. Otherwise, only the local account that Setup Assistant creates is a managed account. This value is available in macOS 11 and later. A dictionary that describes the administrator account to create with Setup Assistant, which uses the first element and ignores additional elements. A dictionary that describes the administrator account to create with Setup Assistant, which uses the first element and ignores additional elements. If `true`, this sets the account attribute to make the account hidden in the Login Window and Users & Groups. Data that contains the pre-created salted PBKDF2 SHA512 password hash for the account. The full name of the user, which defaults to `shortName` if not specified. The short name of the user. If `true`, and you provide values for `PrimaryAccountFullName` or `PrimaryAccountUserName`, Setup Assistant disables editing for the corresponding fields. `DontAutoPopulatePrimaryAccountInfo` must also be 0 (or missing).

If the user's password is also available from authentication through ConfigurationURL, Setup Assistant automatically creates the primary account with that information and skips showing the user interface to view or edit these fields.

This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. If `true`, Setup Assistant ignores the primary account information and requires the user to enter that information. If `false`, Setup Assistant prefills the Full Name field with `PrimaryAccountFullName` and the User Name field with `PrimaryAccountUserName`. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. The account name for the primary account. If present, Setup Assistant uses this value to prefill the User Name field. However, Setup Assistant ignores this value if `DontAutoPopulatePrimaryAccountInfo` is `true`. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. The full name for the primary account. If present, Setup Assistant uses this value to prefill the Full Name field. However, Setup Assistant ignores this value if `DontAutoPopulatePrimaryAccountInfo` is `true`. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. If `true`, Setup Assistant creates the primary accounts as regular users, and you must specify a value for `AutoSetupAdminAccounts`. If `true`, Setup Assistant skips the user interface for setting up primary accounts and disables autologin. If `true`, you must specify a value for `AutoSetupAdminAccounts`. When a macOS (v10.11 and later) device is configured via DEP to enroll in an MDM server and the DEP profile has the await_device_configuration flag set to true, the AccountConfiguration command can be sent to the device to have it create the local administrator account (thereby skipping the page to create this account in Setup Assistant). This command can only be sent to a macOS device that is in the AwaitingConfiguration state.

items.title: Active NSExtensions Command
items.summary: Get a list of active extensions for a user on a device.
items.searchKeys: Extensions ExtensionsItem UserElection Version Path ContainerIdentifier ContainerDisplayName DisplayName ExtensionPoint Identifier FilterExtensionPoints FilterExtensionPointsItem
items.searchContent:
Get a list of active extensions for a user on a device. This command returns information about the active extensions for a user. Extensions exist for each user, not for the device.

Extensions restricted from executing by Application Launch Restrictions or the `NSExtensionManagement` configuration profile won't appear in the response.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of dictionaries that contains information about active extensions on the device. A dictionary that contains information about an extension. The user-selected state of the extension, which a user sets in the Extensions preference pane in System Preferences. The version of the extension. The path to the extension. The identifier of the container. The display name of the container. The extension's display name. The `NSExtensionPointIdentifier` for the extension. The identifier of the extension. An array of extension points. If you choose to provide this value, the response only includes the app extensions for the extension points you specify. Returns information about the active NSExtensions for a particular user.
NSExtensions are installed and enabled at the user level. There is no concept of "device" NSExtensions.
Requires "Query Installed Apps" right; supported on user channel only.

items.title: Install Enterprise Application Command
items.summary: Install an enterprise app on a device.
items.searchKeys: iOSApp ChangeManagementState Configuration ANY ManagementFlags InstallAsManaged PinningRevocationCheckRequired ManifestURLPinningCerts ManifestURLPinningCertsItem ManifestURL Manifest ANY
items.searchContent:
Install an enterprise app on a device. This command provides a more secure version of the `InstallApplication` command when that uses a `ManifestURL`. The request must contain either `Manifest` or `ManifestURL`. Using `Manifest` ignores the pinning options. When using `ManifestURL`, specify the pinning options to increase security. In macOS, the device returns an `Acknowledged` response after validating the parameters, but before downloading and installing the app. However, it doesn't notify the MDM server about errors that occur during the installation process.

This command fails if Declarative Device Management is managing the app.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. If `true`, the app is an iOS app that can run on a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. The change management state. This value doesn't work with the user enrollments. The only possible value is:

- `Managed`: Take management of the app if the user installed it already and `InstallAsManaged` is `true`.

Available in macOS 11 and later. A dictionary that contains the initial configuration of the app, if you choose to provide it. Available in macOS 11 and later. An app configuration. The management flags. The possible values are:

- `1`: If `InstallAsManaged` is `true`, remove the app upon removal of the MDM profile.

Available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, install the app as a managed app. Otherwise, the system installs the app as unmanaged. If you reinstall a manged app and omit this value or set it to `false`, the app becomes unmanaged.

For manifest-based installs, if `true`, the system only considers apps installed in `/Applications` as managed. In macOS 11 through 13, the system requires that the `pkg` only contains a single signed app.

Available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, certificate revocation checks require a positive response when using certificate pinning with `ManifestURLPinningCerts`. An array of DER-encoded certificates to pin the connection when fetching the `ManifestURL`. A certificate in DER-encoded format. The URL of the app manifest, which needs to begin with `https:`. The manifest is returned as a property list that uses the `ManifestURL` format. A dictionary that specifies where to download the app. This value uses the `ManifestURL` format. A dictionary that specifies where to download the app. This value uses the `ManifestURL` format.

items.title: Install Application Command
items.summary: Install a third-party app on a device.
items.searchKeys: RejectionReason State Identifier iOSApp InstallAsManaged ChangeManagementState Attributes Lockable Hideable CellularSliceUUID TapToPayScreenLock Removable AssociatedDomainsEnableDirectDownloads AssociatedDomains AssociatedDomain RelayUUID DNSProxyUUID ContentFilterUUID VPNUUID Configuration ANY ManagementFlags ManifestURL Options PurchaseMethod Identifier iTunesStoreID
items.searchContent:
Install a third-party app on a device. The request must contain only one of these keys: `iTunesStoreID`, `Identifier`, or `ManifestURL`.

Installation prompts the user to approve or cancel the update. If the device is supervised, the device only prompts when the app to install is in the foreground.

Set the organization name that appears in this prompt in the `OrganizationInfo` dictionary using the `Settings` command.

If the app is a managed app, this command updates it. This command fails if Declarative Device Management is managing the app.

In macOS, the device returns an `Acknowledged` response after validating the parameters, but before downloading and installing the app. However, it doesn't notify the MDM server about errors that occur during the installation process.

For macOS VPP app installations, if the app is device licensed, the system must receive the `InstallApplication` command on the device channel. If the app is user licensed, the system must receive the `InstallApplication` command on the user channel.

Prior to iOS 16.0 and tvOS 16.0, this command would return `NotNow` when Setup Assistant was running. Starting in iOS 16.0 and tvOS 16.0, the command may be sent to supervised devices during Setup Assistant. However, you should only attempt to install device-based VPP apps or enterprise apps while in the awaiting configuration state, as it is unlikely the device would have an App Store account configured, and thus commands that depend on one will fail.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The reason, if installation fails. macOS always returns "Other". The app's installation state, if the user accepted the request. If this value is `NeedsRedemption`, the server needs to send a redemption code to complete the app installation. The app's bundle identifier, if the user accepted the request.

> Note:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier is the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone that the watch is paired to. If `true`, the app is an iOS app that can run on a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, install the app as a managed app. Otherwise, the system installs the app as unmanaged. If you reinstall a manged app and omit this value or set it to `false`, the app becomes unmanaged.

For manifest-based installs, if `true`, the system only considers apps installed in `/Applications` as managed. In macOS 11 through 13, the system requires that the `pkg` only contains a single signed app.

Available in macOS 11 and later. The change management state. This value doesn't work with the user enrollment feature introduced in iOS 13, or any type of account driven enrollment. Available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 11 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. The only possible value is:

- `Managed`: Take management of the app if the user installed it already and `InstallAsManaged` is `true`. A dictionary that contains the initial attributes of the app, if you choose to provide it. Available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. If `false`, the system prevents the user from locking the app. This also prevents the user from hiding the app. If `false`, the system prevents the user from hiding the app. It doesn't affect the user's ability to leave it in the App Library, while removing it from the Home Screen. The data network name (DNN) or app category. For DNN, the value is `DNN:name`, where `name` is the carrier-provided DNN name. For app category, the value is `AppCategory:category`, where `category` is a carrier-provided string like "Enterprise1".

Available in iOS 17 and later. If `true`, Tap to Pay on iPhone requires users to use Face ID or a passcode to unlock their device after every transaction that requires a customer's card PIN. If `false`, the user can configure this setting on their device.

Available in iOS 16.4 and later. If `false`, this app isn't removable while it's a managed app. Available in iOS 14 and later, and tvOS 14 and later. If `true`, perform claimed site association verification directly at the domain instead of on Apple's servers. Only set this to `true` for domains that can't access the internet. Available in iOS 14 and later. An array that contains the associated domains to add to this app. Available in iOS 13 and later. The relay UUID for this app. Available in iOS 17 and later. The DNS proxy UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later. The content filter UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later. A per-app VPN unique identifier for this app. Available in iOS 7 and later. A dictionary that contains the initial configuration of the app, if you choose to provide it. Available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 11 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. An app configuration key. A bitwise OR of the management flags. The possible values are:

- `1`: If `InstallAsManaged` is `true`, remove the app upon removal of the MDM profile.
- `4`: Prevent backup of app data.
- `5`: Both `1` and `4`.

Available in iOS 5 and later, macOS 11 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. The URL of the app manifest, which needs to begin with `https:`. The manifest is returned as a property list that uses the `ManifestURL` format. A dictionary that contains the app installation options. The app's purchase type, which must be one of the following values:

- `0`: Free apps and Legacy Volume Purchase Program (VPP) with a redemption code. This option is only available in iOS.
- `1`: Volume Purchase Program (VPP) app assignment.

Set this value to `1` to install first-party apps without user login to the iTunes Store, such as Mail or Safari, or to install an iOS app with user enrollment. The app's bundle identifier.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`. The app's iTunes Store identifier.

The reason, if installation fails. macOS always returns "Other".

notes.content:
The request must contain only one of these keys: `iTunesStoreID`, `Identifier`, or `ManifestURL`.

Installation prompts the user to approve or cancel the update. If the device is supervised, the device only prompts when the app to install is in the foreground.

Set the organization name that appears in this prompt in the `OrganizationInfo` dictionary using the `Settings` command.

If the app is a managed app, this command updates it. This command fails if Declarative Device Management is managing the app.

In macOS, the device returns an `Acknowledged` response after validating the parameters, but before downloading and installing the app. However, it doesn't notify the MDM server about errors that occur during the installation process.

For macOS VPP app installations, if the app is device licensed, the system must receive the `InstallApplication` command on the device channel. If the app is user licensed, the system must receive the `InstallApplication` command on the user channel.

Prior to iOS 16.0 and tvOS 16.0, this command would return `NotNow` when Setup Assistant was running. Starting in iOS 16.0 and tvOS 16.0, the command may be sent to supervised devices during Setup Assistant. However, you should only attempt to install device-based VPP apps or enterprise apps while in the awaiting configuration state, as it is unlikely the device would have an App Store account configured, and thus commands that depend on one will fail.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Installed Application List Command
items.summary: Get a list of the installed apps on a device.
items.searchKeys: InstalledApplicationList InstalledApplicationListItem Source IsAppClip DownloadCancelled DownloadPaused DownloadWaiting DownloadFailed HasUpdateAvailable AdHocCodeSigned BetaApp DeviceBasedVPP AppStoreVendable Installing IsValidated DynamicSize BundleSize Name ShortVersion Version DistributorIdentifier ExternalVersionIdentifier Identifier Items ItemsItem ManagedAppsOnly Identifiers IdentifiersItem
items.searchContent:
Get a list of the installed apps on a device. This command allows the server to query for installed 3rd party applications. The response also includes system apps in macOS, iOS 26 and later, tvOS 26 and later, visionOS 26 and later, and watchOS 26 and later.

This command doesn't return apps that Declarative Device Management is managing if the `ManagedAppsOnly` key is set to `true`, or if the enrollment type is a user enrollment.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see request and response examples for iOS and macOS. An array of dictionaries that describes each installed app. A dictionary that describes an app list item. The source of the application. When the app is managed by Declarative Device Management this value is `Declarative Device Management`. If `true`, the app is an App Clip. Available in iOS 16 and later. If `true`, the user canceled the download. If `true`, the user paused the download. If `true`, the app is in the initial state, which is waiting to download. If `true`, the download failed. If `true`, the app has an update available. This key is present only for App Store apps. In macOS, this key is present only for Volume Purchase Program (VPP) apps. This status updates daily and isn't always up-to-date when installing an app. If `true`, the app is ad-hoc code signed. This query is available in iOS 11.3 and later, and tvOS 11.3 and later. If `true`, the app is part of the Apple Beta Software Program. This value is available in iOS 11.3 and later, and tvOS 11.3 and later. If `true`, installing the app didn't require an Apple Account. This value is available in iOS 11.3 and later, and tvOS 11.3 and later. If `true`, the app came from the App Store and can participate in store features. For device-based Volume Purchase Program (VPP) apps, this value is `false`. This value is available in iOS 11.3 and later, and tvOS 11.3 and later. If `true`, the app is downloading. If `false`, it's already installed. If `true`, the app is valid and can run on the device. If the app is enterprise-distributed and unvalidated, it won't be able to run until validation has occurred. This value is available in iOS 9.2 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. The size of the app's file system in bytes, including the Documents, Library, and other directories. This value is expensive to calculate. Starting in iOS 26, tvOS 26, watchOS 26, and visionOS 26 it isn't present in the response unless it is included in the `Items` request key. This value is available in iOS 5 and later, tvOS 10.2 and later, watchOS 10 and later, and visionOS 1.1 and later. The app's static bundle size, in bytes. This value is expensive to calculate. Starting in iOS 26, macOS 26, tvOS 26, watchOS 26, and visionOS 26 it isn't present in the response unless it is included in the `Items` request key. This value is available in iOS 5 and later, and macOS 10.7 and later, tvOS 10.2 and later, watchOS 10 and later, and visionOS 1.1 and later. The app's name. The app's short version. The app's version. The marketplace hosted application's distributor ID. This value is available in iOS 17.4 and later. The app's external version identifier. You can also retrieve this value from the App Store. For more information, see `Apps and Books for Organizations`.

If the current external version identifier of an app on the App Store doesn't match the external version identifier reported by the device, there may be an app update available for the device.

> Note:
> A newer version of an app might not be available for installation on the device for a variety of reasons. A common reason is that the device's operating system version or hardware is incompatible with the available version of the app. The app's identifier. This key is always be present on iOS and tvOS, but may be missing on macOS.

> Note:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier is the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. An array of strings that represent keys in `InstalledApplicationListItem`. If present, the response only contains the keys listed here, except `Identifier` is always included. If not present, the response contains all keys. Starting in iOS 26, macOS 26, tvOS 26, watchOS 26, and visionOS 26, if this key isn't present, the response omits values that are expensive to calculate.

> Tip:
> Only request the keys that you need, because some key values can take significant time and power to calculate on the device. If `true`, only get a list of managed apps, excluding ones that Declarative Device Management is managing. This value is available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later.

> Note:
> If the enrollment type is a user enrollment, the system always considers this key as set to `true` and only returns managed apps, excluding ones that Declarative Device Management is managing. An array of app identifiers. Provide this value to limit the response to only include these apps. This value is available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, tvOS 10.2 and later, visionOS 1.1 and later, and watchOS 10 and later.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.

items.title: Invite To Program Command
items.summary: Invite a user to join the Volume Purchase Program (VPP).
items.searchKeys: InvitationResult InvitationURL ProgramID
items.searchContent:
Invite a user to join the Volume Purchase Program (VPP). This command allows a server to invite a user to join the Volume Purchase Program (VPP). It issues the invitation, but doesn't allow the server to monitor whether the user joins the program. This command yields a `NotNow` status if Setup Assistant is running.

The command doesn't work with Account Driven enrollments.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The result of the command. The Volume Purchase Program (VPP) invitation URL. The program's identifier, which can only be `com.apple.cloudvpp`.

This command allows a server to invite a user to join the Volume Purchase Program (VPP). It issues the invitation, but doesn't allow the server to monitor whether the user joins the program. This command yields a `NotNow` status if Setup Assistant is running.

The command doesn't work with Account Driven enrollments.

items.title: Managed Application List Command
items.summary: Get the status of all managed apps on a device.
items.searchKeys: ManagedApplicationList ANY app identifier ExternalVersionIdentifier IsValidated HasFeedback HasConfiguration UnusedRedemptionCode ManagementFlags Status Identifiers IdentifiersItem
items.searchContent:
Get the status of all managed apps on a device. This command returns the status of managed apps from the App Store.

Some statuses are transient and the device removes them after reporting them to the server.

This command doesn't return apps that Declarative Device Management is managing.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. A dictionary that contains status information about each managed app. The response doesn't include apps that Declarative Device Management manages. The bundle identifier of the managed app. The app's external version identifier. You can also retrieve this value from the App Store. For more information, see `Apps and Books for Organizations`.

If the current external version identifier of an app on the App Store doesn't match the external version identifier reported by the device, there may be an app update available for the device.

Available in iOS 10.3 and later, macOS 11.3 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later.

> Note:
> A newer version of an app might not be available for installation on the device for a variety of reasons. A common reason is that the device's operating system version or hardware is incompatible with the available version of the app. If 'true', the app is valid and can run on the device. If the app is enterprise-distributed and unvalidated, it won't be able to run until validation has occurred. This value is available in iOS 9.2 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. If 'true', the app has feedback for the server. This value is available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. On macOS 11.3 and later, this value is available if the request was sent on the user channel. If 'true', the app has an update available. This key is present only for App Store apps. In macOS, this key is present only for Volume Purchase Program (VPP) apps. This status updates daily and isn't always up-to-date when installing an app. If the user already purchased a paid app, this code is available for use by another user. This code reports only once. This value is available in iOS 5 and later. The bitwise OR of the following management flags:
* '1': Remove app upon removal of MDM profile.
* '4': Prevent backup of app data. The status of the managed app, which is one of the following values:

- `Queued`: The app is scheduled for installation.
- `NeedsRedemption`: The app needs a redemption code to complete installation.
- `Redeeming`: The device is redeeming the redemption code for the app.
- `Prompting`: The app installation is prompting the user.
- `PromptingForLogin' - The app installation is prompting the user for App Store credentials.
- `ValidatingPurchase`: Validation of the app purchase is occurring.
- `PromptingForUpdate`: An app update is prompting the user.
- `PromptingForUpdateLogin`: An app update is prompting the user for App Store credentials.
- `PromptingForManagement`: Changing the app to a managed app is prompting the user.
- `ValidatingUpdate`: Validation of an app update is occurring.
- `Updating`: The app is updating.
- `Installing`: The app is installing.
- `Managed`: The installed app is a managed app.
- `ManagedButUninstalled`: The app is a managed app and the user removed it. Reinstalling the app reinstates it as a managed app.
- `Unknown`: The app state is unknown.

The following statuses are transient and report only once:

- `UserInstalledApp`: The user installed the app before managed app installation could occur.
- `UserRejected`: The user rejected the offer to install the app.
- `UpdateRejected`: The user rejected the offer to update the app.
- `ManagementRejected`:The user rejected management of an installed app.
- `Failed`: The app installation failed. The bundle identifiers of the managed apps to include in the response.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.

items.title: Apply Redemption Code Command
items.summary: Complete the installation of an app using a redemption code.
items.searchKeys: RedemptionCode Identifier
items.searchContent:
Complete the installation of an app using a redemption code. This command provides a redemption code to complete installing an app. Use this when `InstallApplication` returns `NeedsRedemption`, or when `ManagedApplicationList` returns `NeedsRedemption` for the status of the app.

Sending a redemption code to an app that doesn't need it produces an error.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The redemption code that applies to the app pending installation. The bundle identifier of the app.

items.title: Remove Application Command
items.summary: Remove an app.
items.searchKeys: Identifier
items.searchContent:
Remove an app. This command allows a server to remove managed apps. It also allows a server to remove unmanaged and system deletable apps on supervised devices in iOS 26 and later, tvOS 26 and later, visionOS 26 and later, and watchOS 26 and later. When the device removes an app, it also removes the data for the app.

This command fails for apps that Declarative Device Management is managing.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The bundle identifier of the managed app.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.

items.title: Certificate List Command
items.summary: Get a list of installed certificates on a device.
items.searchKeys: CertificateList CertificateListItem Data IsIdentity CommonName ManagedOnly
items.searchContent:
Get a list of installed certificates on a device. This command allows the server to retrieve the list of installed certificates on the device. The command requires that the server has the Inspect Profile Manifest privilege. For user enrollment, this request returns only certificates pushed by MDM.

This command doesn't return certificates that Declarative Device Management installs. Instead, use the Declarative Device Management `StatusSecurityCertificateList` status item to monitor the Declarative Device Management certificates.

Starting with iOS 15.4, this command returns a Not Now response before the passcode-protected device's first unlock after a device boots. Between iOS 15.0 and iOS 15.4, devices in that state didn't respond with Not Now, but the response might not contain all identity certificates.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of certificate list items that describes each certificate. A dictionary that contains information about a certificate list item. The certificate in DER-encoded X.509 format. If `true`, this is an identity certificate. The certificate's common name. If `true`, only include certificates that MDM installed or that are in the same profile as the MDM payload. User-enrolled devices ignore this value and always only include managed certificates. This value is available in iOS 13 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and tvOS 13 and later.

The server uses this command to turn on the declarative management engine on the device the first time the server sends it. Subsequent commands trigger a declarative management synchronization operation. The base64-encoded declarative management JSON request using a `TokensResponse`. items.title: Activation Lock Bypass Code Command
items.summary: Get the code to bypass Activation Lock on a device.
items.searchKeys: ActivationLockBypassCode
items.searchContent:
Get the code to bypass Activation Lock on a device. This command allows organizations to retrieve the device's bypass code. Organizations can use the bypass code to remove the Activation Lock from supervised devices prior to device activation without knowing the user's personal Apple Account and password.

Supervised devices generate a device-specific Activation Lock bypass code. The activation server verifies this code to bypass Activation Lock on the device. For more information, see `Creating and Using Bypass Codes`.

A device creates a new bypass code when:

- Setting up the device the first time.
- Erasing and not restoring the device from a backup.
- Erasing and restoring the device from a backup from a different device. The Activation Lock bypass code if it's available. Retrieves the Activation Lock bypass code from the device. This bypass code is only available for 15 days after supervision.

declaration.title: Activation Lock Bypass Code Command
declaration.description: Get the code to bypass Activation Lock on a device.

payload.content: Retrieves the Activation Lock bypass code from the device. This bypass code is only available for 15 days after supervision.

responsekeys.content: The Activation Lock bypass code if it's available.

notes.content:
This command allows organizations to retrieve the device's bypass code. Organizations can use the bypass code to remove the Activation Lock from supervised devices prior to device activation without knowing the user's personal Apple Account and password.

Supervised devices generate a device-specific Activation Lock bypass code. The activation server verifies this code to bypass Activation Lock on the device. For more information, see `Creating and Using Bypass Codes`.

A device creates a new bypass code when:

- Setting up the device the first time.
- Erasing and not restoring the device from a backup.
- Erasing and restoring the device from a backup from a different device.

items.title: Device Configured Command

Inform the device that it can allow the user to continue in Setup Assistant. This command only works on Device Enrollment Program (DEP) devices that have their cloud configuration set to await configuration.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. Informs the device that it can continue past DEP enrollment. Only works on devices in DEP that have their cloud configuration set to await configuration.

This command only works on Device Enrollment Program (DEP) devices that have their cloud configuration set to await configuration.

items.title: Erase Device Command
items.summary: Remotely and immediately erase a device.
items.searchKeys: ReturnToService BootstrapToken MDMProfileData WiFiProfileData Enabled ObliterationBehavior PIN DisallowProximitySetup PreserveDataPlan
items.searchTitles: Erase Device Command  Use return to service
items.searchContent:
Remotely and immediately erase a device. This command allows the server to immediately erase a device, even a locked device, without warning the user. The device sends a response to the server, but it doesn't retry if it isn't successful the first time.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The configuration settings for return to service. This value is available in iOS 17 and later, with Shared iPad, in tvOS 18 and later, and in visionOS 26 and later. The bootstrap token the system uses to implement return to service with app preservation. Required when enabling return to service through the cloud configuration. The MDM profile that installs after erasure when using return to service. This key is required for all unsupervised devices, as well as supervised devices that don't enroll with Automated Device Enrollment. If provided, the device uses this profile directly instead of fetching it from the server. For devices that enroll with Automated Device Enrollment, this key isn't necessary unless the cloud configuration profile of the device contains the `configuration-web-url` key.

The cloud configuration still downloads from Apple's servers when the profile contains this key, so the supervision identity, MDM removability, and other settings from the cloud configuration still apply. However, the device doesn't use the specified URL in the cloud configuration to fetch the MDM profile. The Wi-Fi profile that installs after erasure when using return to service. This is required when the device doesn't have an Ethernet or cellular connection. If `true`, the device tries to reenroll itself automatically after erasure. The user needs to deactivate all activation locks for this feature to work correctly. This key defines the fallback behavior for erasing a device.

In macOS 12 and later, this command uses Erase All Content and Settings (EACS) on Mac computers with the Apple M1 chip or the Apple T2 Security Chip. On those devices, if EACS can't run, the device can use obliteration (macOS 11.x behavior). This key has no effect on machines prior to the T2 chip. For a list of supported macs, see [Mac models with the Apple T2 Security Chip](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT208862).

Upon receiving this command, the device performs preflight checks to determine if the device is in a state that allows EACS. The `status` of the `EraseDeviceResponse` is either `Acknowledged` or `Error`.

The following values define the device's fallback behavior:

- `DoNotObliterate`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds to the server with an `Error` status and doesn't attempt to erase itself.
  If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device doesn't attempt to erase itself.
- `ObliterateWithWarning`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds with an `Acknowledged` status and then attempts to erase itself.
    If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device attempts to erase itself.
- `Always`: The system doesn't attempt EACS. T2 and later devices always obliterate.
- `Default`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds to the server with an `Error` status and then attempts to erase itself.
  If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device attempts to erase itself. The six-character PIN for Find My. This value is available in macOS 10.8 and later. If `true`, disable Proximity Setup on the next reboot and skip the pane in Setup Assistant. This value is available in iOS 11 and later. Prior to iOS 14, don't use this option with any other option. If `true`, preserve the data plan on an iPhone or iPad with eSIM functionality, if one exists. This value is available in iOS 11 and later. This command allows the server to remotely erase the device. This command requires the Device Erase right.

payload.content: This command allows the server to remotely erase the device. This command requires the Device Erase right.

payloadkeys.content: If `true`, preserve the data plan on an iPhone or iPad with eSIM functionality, if one exists. This value is available in iOS 11 and later.
If `true`, disable Proximity Setup on the next reboot and skip the pane in Setup Assistant. This value is available in iOS 11 and later. Prior to iOS 14, don't use this option with any other option.
The six-character PIN for Find My. This value is available in macOS 10.8 and later.

This key defines the fallback behavior for erasing a device.

In macOS 12 and later, this command uses Erase All Content and Settings (EACS) on Mac computers with the Apple M1 chip or the Apple T2 Security Chip. On those devices, if EACS can't run, the device can use obliteration (macOS 11.x behavior). This key has no effect on machines prior to the T2 chip. For a list of supported macs, see [Mac models with the Apple T2 Security Chip](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT208862).

Upon receiving this command, the device performs preflight checks to determine if the device is in a state that allows EACS. The `status` of the `EraseDeviceResponse` is either `Acknowledged` or `Error`.

The following values define the device's fallback behavior:

- `DoNotObliterate`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds to the server with an `Error` status and doesn't attempt to erase itself.
  If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device doesn't attempt to erase itself.
- `ObliterateWithWarning`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds with an `Acknowledged` status and then attempts to erase itself.
    If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device attempts to erase itself.
- `Always`: The system doesn't attempt EACS. T2 and later devices always obliterate.
- `Default`: If EACS preflight fails, the device responds to the server with an `Error` status and then attempts to erase itself.
  If EACS preflight succeeds, but EACS fails, the device attempts to erase itself.
The configuration settings for return to service. This value is available in iOS 17 and later, with Shared iPad, in tvOS 18 and later, and in visionOS 26 and later.

subkeys.title: Use return to service
subkeys.content: If `true`, the device tries to reenroll itself automatically after erasure. The user needs to deactivate all activation locks for this feature to work correctly.
The Wi-Fi profile that installs after erasure when using return to service. This is required when the device doesn't have an Ethernet or cellular connection.

The MDM profile that installs after erasure when using return to service. This key is required for all unsupervised devices, as well as supervised devices that don't enroll with Automated Device Enrollment. If provided, the device uses this profile directly instead of fetching it from the server. For devices that enroll with Automated Device Enrollment, this key isn't necessary unless the cloud configuration profile of the device contains the `configuration-web-url` key.

The cloud configuration still downloads from Apple's servers when the profile contains this key, so the supervision identity, MDM removability, and other settings from the cloud configuration still apply. However, the device doesn't use the specified URL in the cloud configuration to fetch the MDM profile.
The bootstrap token the system uses to implement return to service with app preservation. Required when enabling return to service through the cloud configuration.

notes.content:
This command allows the server to immediately erase a device, even a locked device, without warning the user. The device sends a response to the server, but it doesn't retry if it isn't successful the first time.

items.title: Refresh Cellular Plans Command
items.summary: Query a carrier URL for active eSIM cellular-plan profiles on a device.
items.searchKeys: eSIMServerURL
items.searchTitles: Refresh Cellular Plans Command Error codes
items.searchContent:
Query a carrier URL for active eSIM cellular-plan profiles on a device. An error response uses one of the following error codes:

- `36001`: Unable to communicate with the cellular software stack.
- `36002`: The hardware doesn't support this command.
- `36003`: The cellular stack was unable to perform the request. This error can also occur if the cellular stack is busy, in which case, retrying the command later may resolve the issue. The carrier's eSIM server URL to query. Obtain this URL from each carrier separately. Instructs the device to query for active cellular plan eSIM "profiles" (not a profile in the MDM sense)
at the designated carrier eSIM server URL. This command is only supported on cellular devices, and only
a subset of those devices support eSIM configuration management. (Need details from CoreTelephony.)

declaration.title: Refresh Cellular Plans Command
declaration.description: Query a carrier URL for active eSIM cellular-plan profiles on a device.

payload.content:
Instructs the device to query for active cellular plan eSIM "profiles" (not a profile in the MDM sense)
at the designated carrier eSIM server URL. This command is only supported on cellular devices, and only
a subset of those devices support eSIM configuration management. (Need details from CoreTelephony.)

payloadkeys.content: The carrier's eSIM server URL to query. Obtain this URL from each carrier separately.

notes.title: Error codes
notes.content:
An error response uses one of the following error codes:

- `36001`: Unable to communicate with the cellular software stack.
- `36002`: The hardware doesn't support this command.
- `36003`: The cellular stack was unable to perform the request. This error can also occur if the cellular stack is busy, in which case, retrying the command later may resolve the issue.

items.title: Device Lock Command
items.summary: Remotely and immediately lock a device.
items.searchKeys: MessageResult PIN PhoneNumber Message
items.searchContent:
Remotely and immediately lock a device. You can display a message and phone number on the Lock Screen if the user has set a passcode for the device, it isn't a Shared iPad, and it isn't in Lost Mode. In macOS, this command uses the Find My framework to lock a device, and fails if there's no recovery partition on the device.

> Warning:
> Sending this command to a Mac with Apple silicon running a version of macOS before 11.5 deactivates the Mac. To reactivate that Mac, it needs a network connection and authentication by a local administrator with Secure Token enabled.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The message result if the command includes a message or phone number, which is one of the following values:

- `Success`: The message displayed successfully.
- `DeviceInLostMode`: The device is in Lost Mode.
- `NoPasscodeSet`: The message didn't display because there isn't a set passcode.
- `Unknown`: An unknown error occurred. The six-character PIN for Find My. This value is available in macOS 10.8 and later. The phone number to display on the Lock Screen. This value doesn't apply to a Shared iPad device. This value is available in iOS 7 and later and macOS 11.5 and later (for a Mac with Apple silicon only). The message to display on the Lock Screen of the device. This value doesn't apply to a Shared iPad device. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and macOS 10.14 and later. This command allows the server to immediately lock the device. This command requires the Device Lock and Passcode Removal right.

declaration.title: Device Lock Command
declaration.description: Remotely and immediately lock a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to immediately lock the device. This command requires the Device Lock and Passcode Removal right.

payloadkeys.content: The message to display on the Lock Screen of the device. This value doesn't apply to a Shared iPad device. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and macOS 10.14 and later.

The phone number to display on the Lock Screen. This value doesn't apply to a Shared iPad device. This value is available in iOS 7 and later and macOS 11.5 and later (for a Mac with Apple silicon only).
The six-character PIN for Find My. This value is available in macOS 10.8 and later.

responsekeys.content:
The message result if the command includes a message or phone number, which is one of the following values:

- `Success`: The message displayed successfully.
- `DeviceInLostMode`: The device is in Lost Mode.
- `NoPasscodeSet`: The message didn't display because there isn't a set passcode.
- `Unknown`: An unknown error occurred.

notes.content:
You can display a message and phone number on the Lock Screen if the user has set a passcode for the device, it isn't a Shared iPad, and it isn't in Lost Mode. In macOS, this command uses the Find My framework to lock a device, and fails if there's no recovery partition on the device.

> Warning:
> Sending this command to a Mac with Apple silicon running a version of macOS before 11.5 deactivates the Mac. To reactivate that Mac, it needs a network connection and authentication by a local administrator with Secure Token enabled.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Disable Lost Mode Command
items.summary: Take the device out of Lost Mode.
items.searchContent:
Take the device out of Lost Mode. A device responds with error codes:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost Mode.
- `12069`: If the request to disable Lost Mode failed.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

Erasing a device also disables Lost Mode. To reenable Lost Mode, the MDM server needs to store the device's Lost Mode state before erasing it, and restore that state if the device enrolls again.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. This command allows the server to take the device out of MDM lost mode.

declaration.title: Disable Lost Mode Command
declaration.description: Take the device out of Lost Mode.

payload.content: This command allows the server to take the device out of MDM lost mode.

notes.content:
A device responds with error codes:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost Mode.
- `12069`: If the request to disable Lost Mode failed.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

Erasing a device also disables Lost Mode. To reenable Lost Mode, the MDM server needs to store the device's Lost Mode state before erasing it, and restore that state if the device enrolls again.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Enable Lost Mode Command
items.summary: Enable Lost Mode on a device, which provides a message and phone number on the Lock Screen.
items.searchKeys: Footnote PhoneNumber Message
items.searchContent:
Enable Lost Mode on a device, which provides a message and phone number on the Lock Screen. While in Lost Mode, a device responds to invalid commands with error code `12078`.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. If present, the device displays this text at the bottom of the Lock Screen. If present, the device displays this phone number on the Lock Screen. You must provide this value if you don't provide a value for `Message`. If present, the device displays this text on the Lock Screen. You must provide this value if you don't provide a value for `PhoneNumber`. This command allows the server to put the device in MDM lost mode, with a message, phone number, and footnote text. A message or phone number must be provided.

declaration.title: Enable Lost Mode Command
declaration.description: Enable Lost Mode on a device, which provides a message and phone number on the Lock Screen.

payload.content: This command allows the server to put the device in MDM lost mode, with a message, phone number, and footnote text. A message or phone number must be provided.

payloadkeys.content: If present, the device displays this text on the Lock Screen. You must provide this value if you don't provide a value for `PhoneNumber`.
If present, the device displays this phone number on the Lock Screen. You must provide this value if you don't provide a value for `Message`.
If present, the device displays this text at the bottom of the Lock Screen.

notes.content:
While in Lost Mode, a device responds to invalid commands with error code `12078`.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Device Location Command
items.summary: Request the location of a device when in Lost Mode.
items.searchKeys: Timestamp Course Speed Altitude VerticalAccuracy HorizontalAccuracy Longitude Latitude
items.searchContent:
Request the location of a device when in Lost Mode. A device responds with error codes:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost mode.
- `12068`: If its location is unknown.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The RFC 3339 timestamp of when the server determines the location of the device. The direction the device is traveling, which is a negative value if the course is unknown. The speed of the device in meters per second, which is a negative value if the speed is unknown. The altitude of the device's location, which is a negative value if the altitude is unknown. The accuracy of the altitude value in meters, which is a negative value if the vertical accuracy is unknown. The radius of uncertainty for the location in meters, which is a negative value if the horizontal accuracy is unknown. The longitude of the device's location. The latitude of the device's location.

declaration.title: Device Location Command
declaration.description: Request the location of a device when in Lost Mode.

responsekeys.content: The latitude of the device's location.
The longitude of the device's location.
The radius of uncertainty for the location in meters, which is a negative value if the horizontal accuracy is unknown.
The accuracy of the altitude value in meters, which is a negative value if the vertical accuracy is unknown.
The altitude of the device's location, which is a negative value if the altitude is unknown.
The speed of the device in meters per second, which is a negative value if the speed is unknown.
The direction the device is traveling, which is a negative value if the course is unknown.
The RFC 3339 timestamp of when the server determines the location of the device.

notes.content:
A device responds with error codes:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost mode.
- `12068`: If its location is unknown.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Play Lost Mode Sound Command
items.summary: Play the Lost Mode sound on a device that's in Lost Mode.
items.searchContent:
Play the Lost Mode sound on a device that's in Lost Mode. A device responds with error code:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost mode.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

The sound plays until the server disables Lost Mode or the user disables the sound on the device.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. This command allows the server to tell the device to play a sound if it is in MDM Lost Mode. The sound will play until the device is either removed from Lost Mode or a user disables the sound from the device.

declaration.title: Play Lost Mode Sound Command
declaration.description: Play the Lost Mode sound on a device that's in Lost Mode.

payload.content: This command allows the server to tell the device to play a sound if it is in MDM Lost Mode. The sound will play until the device is either removed from Lost Mode or a user disables the sound from the device.

notes.content:
A device responds with error code:

- `12067`: If it isn't in Lost mode.
- `12078`: If the command is invalid while in Lost Mode.

The sound plays until the server disables Lost Mode or the user disables the sound on the device.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Restart Device Command
items.summary: Remotely and immediately restart a device.
items.searchKeys: NotifyUser KextPaths KextPathsItem RebuildKernelCache
items.searchContent:
Remotely and immediately restart a device. A passcode-locked iOS device doesn't rejoin a Wi-Fi network after restarting, so it may not be able to communicate with the server.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. If `true`, notifies the user to restart the device at their convenience. No forced restart occurs unless the device is at `loginwindow` with no logged-in users. The user can dismiss the notification and ignore the request. No further notifications display unless you resend the command.

This value is available in macOS 11.3 and later. If `RebuildKernelCache` is `true`, this value specifies the paths to kexts to add to the auxiliary kernel cache since the last kernel cache rebuild. If not present, the system only adds previously discovered kexts to the kernel cache. This value is available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, the system rebuilds the kernel cache during a device restart. If `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response, the device requests the bootstrap token from the MDM server prior to executing this command. This value is available in macOS 11 and later. This command requires the Device Lock access right. The device will restart immediately.

declaration.title: Restart Device Command
declaration.description: Remotely and immediately restart a device.

payload.content: This command requires the Device Lock access right. The device will restart immediately.

payloadkeys.content: If `true`, the system rebuilds the kernel cache during a device restart. If `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response, the device requests the bootstrap token from the MDM server prior to executing this command. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.

If `RebuildKernelCache` is `true`, this value specifies the paths to kexts to add to the auxiliary kernel cache since the last kernel cache rebuild. If not present, the system only adds previously discovered kexts to the kernel cache. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.

If `true`, notifies the user to restart the device at their convenience. No forced restart occurs unless the device is at `loginwindow` with no logged-in users. The user can dismiss the notification and ignore the request. No further notifications display unless you resend the command.

This value is available in macOS 11.3 and later.

notes.content:
A passcode-locked iOS device doesn't rejoin a Wi-Fi network after restarting, so it may not be able to communicate with the server.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Clear Restrictions Password Command
items.summary: Clear the Screen Time password and the restrictions on a device.
items.searchContent:
Clear the Screen Time password and the restrictions on a device. In iOS 11 and earlier, this command clears the restrictions password and all restrictions that the password protects.

In iOS 12.2 and later, if Screen Time uses iCloud to share its settings (Share Across Devices), this command disables Screen Time entirely and clears its restrictions. If the user is a child in an iCloud family, the command fails. Otherwise, if Screen Time isn't using iCloud, this command clears the passcode, but not the restrictions, and it leaves Screen Time enabled.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

declaration.title: Clear Restrictions Password Command
declaration.description: Clear the Screen Time password and the restrictions on a device.

notes.content:
In iOS 11 and earlier, this command clears the restrictions password and all restrictions that the password protects.

In iOS 12.2 and later, if Screen Time uses iCloud to share its settings (Share Across Devices), this command disables Screen Time entirely and clears its restrictions. If the user is a child in an iCloud family, the command fails. Otherwise, if Screen Time isn't using iCloud, this command clears the passcode, but not the restrictions, and it leaves Screen Time enabled.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

A dictionary that contains dictionaries of restrictions from each profile. This value is only available when `ProfileRestrictions` is `true` in the command. The keys are the identifiers of the profiles. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6.1 and later. The profile identifiers. This dictionary is only available if `ProfileRestrictions` is `true` in the command. A dictionary of unioned profile restrictions. Unioned restrictions indicate that new restrictions can add to the set. The unioned restriction parameters. The values of the restriction. A dictionary of intersected profile restrictions. Intersected restrictions indicate that new restrictions can only reduce the number of strings in the set. The intersected restriction parameters. The values of the restriction. A dictionary of numeric profile restrictions. The numeric restriction parameters. The value of the restriction. A dictionary of Boolean profile restrictions. The Boolean restriction parameters. The value of the restriction. A dictionary that contains the global restrictions in effect. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6.1 and later. If `true`, the device reports restrictions from each profile. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6.1 and later. This command allows the server to determine what restrictions are being enforced on the device, and the total sum of all restrictions. This command requires the Restrictions Query access right. This technically does work on macOS but it returns a blank dictionary and there no plans to change this behavior.

items.title: Shut Down Device Command
items.summary: Remotely and immediately shut down a device.
items.searchContent:
Remotely and immediately shut down a device. A passcode-locked iOS device doesn't rejoin a Wi-Fi network after a user restarts it and before they unlock it for the first time, so it can't communicate with the server if it needs Wi-Fi to do so.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. This command requires the Device Lock access right. The device will shut down immediately.

declaration.title: Shut Down Device Command
declaration.description: Remotely and immediately shut down a device.

notes.content:
A passcode-locked iOS device doesn't rejoin a Wi-Fi network after a user restarts it and before they unlock it for the first time, so it can't communicate with the server if it needs Wi-Fi to do so.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Content Caching Information Command
items.summary: Get the status of the content caches on a device.
items.searchKeys: StatusResponse TotalBytesStoredFromPeers TotalBytesStoredFromParents TotalBytesStoredFromOrigin TotalBytesReturnedToPeers TotalBytesReturnedToClients TotalBytesReturnedToChildren TotalBytesImported TotalBytesDropped TotalBytesAreSince TetheratorStatus StartupStatus ServerGUID RestrictedMedia RegistrationStatus RegistrationStarted RegistrationResponseCode RegistrationError PublicAddress PrivateAddresses PrivateAddressesItem Port PersonalCacheUsed PersonalCacheLimit PersonalCacheFree Peers PeersItem version port healthy guid friendly details local-network wired speed is-portable capabilities ur sc query-parameters pc ns im cache-size ac-power alert peerAddress addresses address postDate className address Parents ParentsItem version port healthy guid details local-network wired speed is-portable capabilities ur sc query-parameters pc ns im cache-size ac-power alert addresses address postDate className address MaxCachePressureLast1Hour DataMigrationProgress DataMigrationError userInfo ANY code domain DataMigrationCompleted CacheUsed CacheStatus CacheLimit CacheFree CacheDetails Category Name Alerts AlertsItem pathPreventingAccess resource reservedVolumeSpace cacheLimit postDate className AlertsForPeerFilterRanges ANY index addresses address peerFilterRangeIndex postDate className ActualCacheUsed Active Activated
items.searchContent:
Get the status of the content caches on a device. A dictionary that contains the status of content caching on a device. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache saved from peer content caches since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache saved from parent content caches since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache saved from the internet since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache served to peer content caches since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache served to client iOS, macOS, and tvOS devices since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache served to its child content cache since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache received since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The amount of data, in bytes, that the content cache downloaded, but couldn't add to its cache, since the `TotalBytesAreSince` date. The start date to use when collecting data for the other `TotalBytes` values. The status of tethered caching, which is content caching with a shared internet connection, which is one of the following values:

- `-1:` Unknown
-  `0:` Disabled
-  `1:` Enabled The status of the content cache's registration with Apple. The unique identifier of the content cache. If `true`, a restriction prevents caching of certain content types. The status of the content cache's registration with Apple, which is one of the following values:

- `-1:` Failed
-  `0:` Pending
-  `1:` Succeeded The date when the content cache began registering itself with Apple. This value is only available during registration attempts. If present, the HTTP response code the content cache received when it failed to register itself with Apple. If present, the reason the content cache failed to register itself with Apple. The public IP address of the content cache. An array of the content cache's local IP addresses. Local IP address at which the Content Cache listens for requests from clients, peers, and children. The IP port number the content cache listens to for requests from clients, peers, and children. The amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache for personal iCloud content. The maximum amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache for personal iCloud content. A value of `0` indicates an unlimited amount. The amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache for personal iCloud content. An array of dictionaries that describes peer content caches. A dictionary that describes a peer content cache. The version number of the peer content cache software. The IP port number the peer content cache listens to for requests. If `true`, the peer content cache is able to respond to requests from the content cache. The unique identifier of the peer content cache. If `true`, the peer content cache is able to respond to requests from the content cache. A dictionary that contains additional details about the peer content cache. A dictionary that describes the peer content cache's connection to its local network. If `true`, the peer content cache has a wired connection to its local network. If `false`, it has a wireless connection; for example, Wi-Fi. The transfer speed, in megabits per second, of the peer content cache's connection to its local network. If `true`, the peer content cache computer is portable; for example, a laptop. A dictionary that describes the capabilities of the peer content cache. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of prioritizing imports and uploads. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of caching shared non-iCloud content. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of handling query parameters in URLs. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of caching personal iCloud content. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of handling namespaces, which is an aspect of personal caching. If `true`, the peer content cache is capable of imports and uploads. The maximum amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the peer content cache. If `true`, the peer content cache power source is AC; otherwise, an internal battery provides its power. A dictionary that describes an alert related to the peer content cache. The local IP address of a peer content cache. An array of local IP addresses of peer content caches. Local IP address of a peer Content Cache. The date of the alert. The type of the alert. The local IP address of the peer content cache. An array of dictionaries that describes parent content caches. A dictionary that describes a parent content cache. The version number of the parent content cache software. The IP port number the parent content cache listens to for requests. If `true,` the parent content cache is able to respond to requests from this content cache. The unique identifier of the parent content cache. A dictionary that contains additional details about the parent content cache. A dictionary that describes the parent content cache's connection to its local network. If `true`, the parent content cache has a wired connection to its local network. If `false`, it has a wireless connection; for example, Wi-Fi. The transfer speed, in megabits per second, of the parent content cache's connection to its local network. If `true`, the parent content cache computer is portable; for example, a laptop. A dictionary that describes the capabilities of the parent content cache. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of prioritizing imports and uploads. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of caching shared non-iCloud content. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of handling query parameters in URLs. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of caching personal iCloud content. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of handling namespaces, which is an aspect of personal caching. If `true`, the parent content cache is capable of imports and uploads. The maximum amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the parent content cache. If `true`, the parent content cache power source is AC; otherwise, an internal battery provides its power. A dictionary that describes an alert related to the parent content cache. An array of local IP addresses of parent content caches. Local IP address of a parent Content Cache. The date of the alert. The type of the alert. The local IP address of the parent content cache. A floating-point number between `0.0` and `1.0` that represents how often the cache needed more disk space over the last hour of operation. A lower value is better. A floating-point number between `0.0` and `1.0` that indicates the percentage of progress in moving the content cache from one volume to another. A value of `1.0` indicates that the content cache has fully migrated. The error that occurred while the content cache moved from one volume to another. A dictionary that contains additional information about the error. A dictionary that contains additional details about the error. The error code. The error domain. If `true`, the content cache finished moving from one volume to another. The amount of disk space, in bytes, cached content uses. Content caching allocates space in its cache for entire files even when it stores only part of those files in its cache. The level of cache pressure. `LowSpace` means cache pressure is high. The maximum amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache. A value of `0` indicates an unlimited amount. This value corresponds to `CacheLimit` in the installed `ContentCaching` profile. The amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache. The amount of disk space that various categories of cached content use. Apple defines these categories and they're subject to change. The amount of disk space, in bytes, that this category of cached content uses. An array that contains the error conditions the content cache detected that aren't related to peer filter ranges, parent content caches, or peer content caches.

See `AlertsForPeerFilterRanges` for errors related to peer filter ranges.

See `Parents` and `Peers` for errors related to parent and peer content caches.

To display these alerts on the device, set `DisplayAlerts` to `true` in the installed `ContentCaching` profile. A dictionary that describes an alert from the content cache. The subpath of the resource that was missing or inaccessible at the time of the alert. This value only applies to the `AssetCacheResourceMissingAlert` type. The resource that was missing or inaccessible at the time of the alert. This value only applies to the `AssetCacheResourceMissingAlert` type. The space, in bytes, that the system reserves at the time of the alert. This value only applies to the `AssetCacheLowSpaceAlert` and `AssetCacheNoSpaceAlert` types. The limit, in bytes, for the content cache at the time of the alert. This value only applies to `AssetCacheLowSpaceAlert` and `AssetCacheNoSpaceAlert` types. The date of the alert. The type of the alert. The error conditions the content cache detected in the `PeerFilterRanges` in the installed `com.apple.AssetCache.managed` payload.

To display these alerts on the device, set `DisplayAlerts` to `true` in the installed `ContentCaching` profile. A dictionary that describes the alerts for the peer filter ranges. The key name is the index into the `PeerFilterRanges` array in the installed `com.apple.AssetCache.managed` payload. An array of local IP addresses of peer content caches that rejected requests from the content cache. Local IP address of a peer Content Cache that rejected requests from this Content Cache. The index into the `PeerFilterRanges` in the installed ContentCaching payload. The date of the alert. The type of the alert. The actual amount of disk space, in bytes, that cached content uses. See related values `CacheUsed` and `PersonalCacheUsed`. If `true`, content caching is ready to serve requests. If `true`, the device has enabled content caching. Enabling content caching doesn't guarantee service. See the `Active` key for the readiness of content caching to serve requests. This command allows the server to query for information about Content Caching.

declaration.title: Content Caching Information Command
declaration.description: Get the status of the content caches on a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to query for information about Content Caching.

responsekeys.content: A dictionary that contains the status of content caching on a device.

subkeys.content: If `true`, the device has enabled content caching. Enabling content caching doesn't guarantee service. See the `Active` key for the readiness of content caching to serve requests.
If `true`, content caching is ready to serve requests.
The actual amount of disk space, in bytes, that cached content uses. See related values `CacheUsed` and `PersonalCacheUsed`.

To display these alerts on the device, set `DisplayAlerts` to `true` in the installed `ContentCaching` profile.

See `AlertsForPeerFilterRanges` for errors related to peer filter ranges.

To display these alerts on the device, set `DisplayAlerts` to `true` in the installed `ContentCaching` profile.

The amount of disk space that various categories of cached content use. Apple defines these categories and they're subject to change.

The amount of disk space, in bytes, available to the content cache.
