items.searchTitles: Device Information Command DeviceInformation attestation hardware support
items.searchContent:
Get detailed information about a device. The following table indicates which System on Chips (SoCs) support DeviceInformation attestation. Unsupported devices ignore the DevicePropertiesAttestation and DeviceAttestationNonce keys. | Support status | iPhone, iPad | Mac | Apple TV | Apple Watch | Vision Pro | |----------------|--------------------------------------|---------------|-------------------------|----------------|------------| | Unsupported | A10x Fusion and earlier | Intel | A10x Fusion and earlier | S3 and earlier | none | | Supported | A11 Bionic and later
All M series | Apple Silicon | A12 Bionic and later | S4 and later | All | Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. A dictionary that contains information about the device. Specifies whether the device can perform an `EraseDeviceCommand` using Erase All Content and Settings (EACS), which is one of the following values: - `success`: The device supports EACS. - `not supported`: The device is too old to support EACS. - `unknown failure`: A problem occurred for which there isn't a more specific error message. - `(other string)`: A reason why the device can't perform EACS, such as "System is not sealed" The key to get an attestation of the device's properties. Available in iOS 16 and later, macOS 14 and later, tvOS 16 and later, and watchOS 10 and later. The hardware requirements for attestation are described below. The value is an array of certificates in DER form that forms a certificate chain. The chain is rooted with the Apple CA `Apple Enterprise Attestation Root CA`. The first array item is the leaf certificate. The leaf certificate contains custom OIDs describing a device. The OS version of the device, and the type of enrollment, determine which OIDs are present in the certificate. If Apple's attestation servers are unable to verify a device property they generate a blank value, omit the OID entirely, or refuse to issue an attestation certificate. The following OIDs were introduced in iOS 16, iPadOS 16, tvOS 16, watchOS 10, visionOS 1 and macOS 14: - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.1` serial number: This is the serial number of the device. It is omitted if the enrollment is a user enrollment. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.2` UDID: For a Mac this has the same value as the `ProvisioningUDID` key, and does not match the UDID used elsewhere in the MDM protocol. It is omitted if the enrollment is a user enrollment. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.2` sepOS version: This is the version of the operating system running on the Secure Enclave when the attestation is generated. Typically this matches the version of the main operating system. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.11.1` Freshness code: This is the freshness code. See the `DeviceAttestationNonce`. This may not match the requested freshness code if a cached attestation was returned. The following OIDs were introduced in iOS 17.2, iPadOS 17.2, tvOS 17.2, watchOS 10.2, visionOS 1.l0, and macOS 14.2: - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.4` Software Update Device ID: This is an identifier of the device model. It is expected to match the `SoftwareUpdateDeviceID` in the `DeviceInformation`` response. This is the device identifier to use when looking up available OS updates through . - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.1` OS Version: This is the version of iOS, iPadOS or tvOS running on the device when the attestation is generated. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.3` LLB Version: This is the version of the Low Level Bootloader firmware running on the device when the attestation is generated. For more information about the boot process, see the documentation of the boot process in the Apple Platform Security guide. The following OIDs were introduced in macOS 14.2: - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.1` System Integrity Protection (SIP) status: This indicates whether SIP is enabled or disabled when the attestation is generated. `0` indicates enabled, `1` indicates disabled. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.2` Secure boot status: This describes part of the configuration of the LocalPolicy when the attestation is generated. The values are `Full Security`, `Reduced Security`, or `Permissive Security`. For a description of these values see the Apple Platform Security guide. - `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.3` Third party kernel extensions allowed: This indicates whether third party kernel extensions are allowed. A value of `0` indicates third party kernel extensions are not allowed. Any other value means that some kinds of third party kernel extensions are allowed. The current state of settable accessibility settings. Available in iOS 16 and later. If `true`, the device has enabled grayscale display. If `true`, the device has enabled zoom. If `true`, the device has enabled voiceover. If `true`, the device has enabled touch accommodations. The accessibility text size apps that support dynamic text use. 0 is the smallest value, and 11 is the largest available. `-1` indicates that the current size is unknown or hasn't been explicitly set. If `true`, the device has enabled reduced transparency. If `true`, the device has enabled reduced motion. If `true`, the device has enabled increase contrast. If `true`, the device has enabled bold text. The device settings that control which updates appear in the Software Update pane in Settings. Available in iOS 14.5 and later. Which software updates to present to the user. - `0`: Allows all updates (the default value). - `1`: Allows only older updates. - `2`: Allows only newer updates. No effect if the device qualifies for only a single update. The device identifier to look up available OS updates through [https://gdmf.apple.com/v2/pmv](https://gdmf.apple.com/v2/pmv). Available in iOS 15 and later, and macOS 12 and later. If `true`, the device supports iOS or iPadOS app installs through MDM. Available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, the `DeviceLockCommand` requires a PIN. Available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, the `EraseDeviceCommand` requires a PIN. Available in macOS 11 and later. The contents of ``ServiceSubscriptionProperty``. Requires the Network Information access right. Properties of this Service Subscription. See below. The name of the home carrier network. This value is available in iOS 16 and later. The description of the slot that contains the SIM representing this subscription. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The raw phone number without punctuation and including country code. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The device Mobile Equipment Identifier (MEID) number. This query is available in iOS 12 and later. The unique identifier for this subscription. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The label of this subscription. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. If `true`, this subscription is the preference for voice. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. If `true`, the phone is roaming. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. If `true`, this subscription is the preference for data. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The device International Mobile Equipment Identity (IMEI) number. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The eSIM identifier. This value is available in iOS 14 and later. The integrated circuit card identifier (ICCID) value. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The current mobile network code (MNC). This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The current mobile country code (MCC). This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The name of the current carrier network. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The version of the carrier settings. This value is available in iOS 12 and later. The current mobile network code (MNC). Requires the Network Information access right. Available as of iOS 4 and deprecated in iOS 16. The current mobile country code (MCC). Requires the Network Information access right. Available as of iOS 4 and deprecated in iOS 16. The key to get the home Mobile Network Code (MNC). Requires the Network Information access right. Available as of iOS 4.2.6 and deprecated in iOS 16. The home Mobile Country Code (MCC). Requires the Network Information access right. Available as of iOS 4.2.6 and deprecated in iOS 16. Apple no longer supports this query. Use `SubscriberMNC` instead. Apple no longer supports this query. Use `SubscriberMCC` instead. If `true`, the device is roaming. Requires the Network Information access right. IAvailable as of iOS 4.2 and deprecated in iOS 16. declaration.title: Device Information Command
declaration.description: Get detailed information about a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to query for specific device information. It's supported in the user channel.

payloadkeys.content: An array of query dictionaries to get information about a device.

subkeys.content: A query dictionary to get information about a device.

payloadkeys.content:
A freshness code that appears in the resulting attestation. This value can contain up to 32 bytes of data. If specified, queries need to contain `DevicePropertiesAttestation`.

The MDM server uses this value to prove that an attestation was recently generated. The system caches the most recently generated attestation on the device. If omitted or if the value matches the cached attestation, the system returns the cached attestation. To request a new attestation, provide a new freshness code. Requests for new attestations are rate limited. If it is fewer than 7 days since the system generated an attestation, the device returns the cached attestation rather than generating a new one.

Available in iOS 16 and later, macOS 14 and later, tvOS 16 and later, and watchOS 10 and later. The hardware requirements for attestation are described below.

The key to get an attestation of the device's properties. Available in iOS 16 and later, macOS 14 and later, tvOS 16 and later, and watchOS 10 and later. The hardware requirements for attestation are described below.

The value is an array of certificates in DER form that forms a certificate chain. The chain is rooted with the Apple CA `Apple Enterprise Attestation Root CA`. The first array item is the leaf certificate. The leaf certificate contains custom OIDs describing a device. The OS version of the device, and the type of enrollment, determine which OIDs are present in the certificate. If Apple's attestation servers are unable to verify a device property they generate a blank value, omit the OID entirely, or refuse to issue an attestation certificate.

The following OIDs were introduced in iOS 16, iPadOS 16, tvOS 16, watchOS 10, visionOS 1 and macOS 14:

- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.1` serial number: This is the serial number of the device. It is omitted if the enrollment is a user enrollment.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.2` UDID: For a Mac this has the same value as the `ProvisioningUDID` key, and does not match the UDID used elsewhere in the MDM protocol. It is omitted if the enrollment is a user enrollment.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.2` sepOS version: This is the version of the operating system running on the Secure Enclave when the attestation is generated. Typically this matches the version of the main operating system.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.11.1` Freshness code: This is the freshness code. See the `DeviceAttestationNonce`. This may not match the requested freshness code if a cached attestation was returned.

The following OIDs were introduced in iOS 17.2, iPadOS 17.2, tvOS 17.2, watchOS 10.2, visionOS 1.l0, and macOS 14.2:

- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.9.4` Software Update Device ID: This is an identifier of the device model. It is expected to match the `SoftwareUpdateDeviceID` in the `DeviceInformation`` response. This is the device identifier to use when looking up available OS updates through <https://gdmf.apple.com/v2/pmv>.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.1` OS Version: This is the version of iOS, iPadOS or tvOS running on the device when the attestation is generated.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.10.3` LLB Version: This is the version of the Low Level Bootloader firmware running on the device when the attestation is generated. For more information about the boot process, see the documentation of the boot process in the Apple Platform Security guide.

The following OIDs were introduced in macOS 14.2:

- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.1` System Integrity Protection (SIP) status: This indicates whether SIP is enabled or disabled when the attestation is generated. `0` indicates enabled, `1` indicates disabled.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.2` Secure boot status: This describes part of the configuration of the LocalPolicy when the attestation is generated. The values are `Full Security`, `Reduced Security`, or `Permissive Security`. For a description of these values see the Apple Platform Security guide.
- `1.2.840.113635.100.8.13.3` Third party kernel extensions allowed: This indicates whether third party kernel extensions are allowed. A value of `0` indicates third party kernel extensions are not allowed. Any other value means that some kinds of third party kernel extensions are allowed.

Specifies whether the device can perform an `EraseDeviceCommand` using Erase All Content and Settings (EACS), which is one of the following values:
- `success`: The device supports EACS.
- `not supported`: The device is too old to support EACS.
- `unknown failure`: A problem occurred for which there isn't a more specific error message.
- `(other string)`: A reason why the device can't perform EACS, such as "System is not sealed"

notes.content: Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

notes.title: DeviceInformation attestation hardware support
notes.content:
The following table indicates which System on Chips (SoCs) support DeviceInformation attestation. Unsupported devices ignore the DevicePropertiesAttestation and DeviceAttestationNonce keys. | Support status | iPhone, iPad | Mac | Apple TV | Apple Watch | Vision Pro | |----------------|--------------------------------------|---------------|-------------------------|----------------|------------| | Unsupported | A10x Fusion and earlier | Intel | A10x Fusion and earlier | S3 and earlier | none | | Supported | A11 Bionic and later
All M series | Apple Silicon | A12 Bionic and later | S4 and later | All |

items.title: Security Info Command
items.summary: Get security-related information about a device.
items.searchKeys: SecurityInfo IsRecoveryLockEnabled BootstrapTokenRequiredForKernelExtensionApproval BootstrapTokenRequiredForSoftwareUpdate BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication AuthenticatedRootVolumeEnabled RemoteDesktopEnabled SecureBoot ReducedSecurity ReducedSecurityItems AllowsMDM AllowsUserKextApproval AllowsAnyAppleSignedOS ExternalBootLevel SecureBootLevel ManagementStatus IsActivationLockManageable IsUserEnrollment UserApprovedEnrollment EnrolledViaDEP FirmwarePasswordStatus AllowOroms ChangePending PasswordExists FirewallSettings LoggingOption LoggingEnabled Applications ApplicationsItem Name BundleID Allowed StealthMode BlockAllIncoming FirewallEnabled SystemIntegrityProtectionEnabled FDE_PersonalRecoveryKeyDeviceKey FDE_PersonalRecoveryKeyCMS FDE_HasInstitutionalRecoveryKey FDE_HasPersonalRecoveryKey FDE_Enabled AutoLockTime PasscodeLockGracePeriodEnforced PasscodeLockGracePeriod PasscodeCompliantWithProfiles PasscodeCompliant PasscodePresent HardwareEncryptionCaps
items.searchContent:
Get security-related information about a device. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. A dictionary that contains security-related information. If `true`, a password is required to enter recovery (see `SetRecoveryLockCommand`). Available in macOS 11.5 and later and only on a Mac with Apple silicon. If `true`, the device can accept a Bootstrap Token from the MDM server instead of prompting for user authentication prior to enabling kernel extensions. This includes enabling kexts through the `com.apple.syspolicy.kernel-extension-policy` payload or triggering the `RestartDevice` command with `RebuildKernelCache` set to `true`. This only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment. If `true`, the device can accept a Bootstrap Token from the MDM server instead of prompting for user authentication prior to installation. This only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment. This value specifies whether the Secure Enclave Processor (SEP) supports and allows secure operations to use the Bootstrap Token. The value is automatically set for devices enrolled through the Device Enrollment Program (DEP). The user can also manually set this value in the RecoveryOS.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment. If `true`, the system booted using an Authenticated Root Volume. This value is available in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, Remote Desktop is active on the device. This value is available in macOS 10.14.4 and later. A dictionary that contains the device's Secure Boot settings. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. Reports which security features the user disables in `recoveryOS`. This property is only present for a Mac with Apple silicon when `SecureBootLevel` is `medium`.

Available in iOS 11 and later. If 'true', the MDM server controls kernel extensions and software updates. If 'true', the user has control over kernel extensions. If 'true', allows any signed version of trusted system software from Apple to run. The device's external boot level, which indicates whether it allows booting from an external device, disallows it, or doesn't support it. The security level for the bootable operating system versions. A dictionary that contains the status of the device's MDM enrollment. If `true`, the type of enrollment allows the MDM to manage Activation Lock for this device. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later. If `true`, the device is user-enrolled. This value is available in iOS 13 and later, and macOS 10.15 and later. If `true`, the enrollment was user-approved. If `false`, the device may reject certain security-sensitive payloads or commands. This value is available in macOS 10.13.2 and later. If `true`, the device enrolled in MDM through the Device Enrollment Program (DEP). This value is available in macOS 10.13.2 and later. A dictionary that contains the status of the EFI firmware password. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later. If `true`, enable ROMs. If `true`, a firmware password change is pending. A device restart is necessary for this change to take effect. Until then, additional attempts to change the password fail.

> Note:
> If `true`, the other values show the current state of the device, not the state after a restart. If `true`, the device has an EFI firmware password. A dictionary that contains the firewall settings. This value is available in macOS 10.12 and later. The type of logging emitted by the firewall. If `true`, logging is enabled. An array of dictionaries that describes the allowed applications. A dictionary that describes the allowed apps. The app's display name if it's determinable from the `BundleID`. The app's bundle identifier. If `true`, the app is an allowed app. If true, stealth mode is active for the firewall. If `true`, the firewall blocks all incoming connections. If `true`, the firewall is on. If `true`, System Integrity Protection (SIP) is active on the device. This value is available in macOS 10.12 and later. If the FileVault personal recovery key has enabled escrow with a recovery key, this value is the device serial number. This is the value that displays to the user at the EFI Login Window as part of the help message if they enter their password incorrectly three times. The server also uses this value as an index when saving the device personal recovery key. This replaces the `recordNumber` that the server returned in the previous escrow mechanism. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later. If the FileVault personal recovery key has enabled escrow with a recovery key, this value contains the key. The certificate from the `FDERecoveryKeyEscrow` profile encrypts the key and wraps it as CMS data. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later. If `true`, FileVault FDE has an institutional recovery key. This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later. If `true`, FileVault FDE has a personal recovery key. This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later. If `true`, the device has enabled FileVault full disk encryption (FDE). This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later. The number of seconds before a device goes to sleep after being idle. This value is only available on Shared iPad in iOS 17 and later. The enforced value for the number of seconds before a locked screen requires the device passcode to unlock it. If a device has a passcode, changing `PasscodeLockGracePeriod` to a larger value doesn't take effect until the user logs out or removes the passcode. This value is only available for Shared iPad. The user preference for the number of seconds before a locked screen requires the device passcode to unlock it. This value is only available for Shared iPad. If `true`, the user's passcode is compliant with requirements from profiles. This key doesn't apply to User-Enrolled devices. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later. If `true`, the user's passcode is compliant with all requirements on the device, including Exchange and other accounts. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later. If `true`, the device has a passcode. This key doesn't apply to User-Enrolled devices. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later. An integer that indicates the underlying hardware encryption capabilities of the device, which is one of the following values:

- `1`: Block-level encryption
- `2`: File-level encryption
- `3`: Both block-level and file-level encryption

> Important:
> For a device to have data protection, `HardwareEncryptionCaps` must be `3` and `PasscodePresent` must `true`.

This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later. This command queries the device for security-related information. Queries are available if the MDM host has the Security Query right.

declaration.title: Security Info Command
declaration.description: Get security-related information about a device.

payload.content: This command queries the device for security-related information. Queries are available if the MDM host has the Security Query right.

responsekeys.content: A dictionary that contains security-related information.

subkeys.content:
An integer that indicates the underlying hardware encryption capabilities of the device, which is one of the following values:

- `1`: Block-level encryption
- `2`: File-level encryption
- `3`: Both block-level and file-level encryption

> Important:
> For a device to have data protection, `HardwareEncryptionCaps` must be `3` and `PasscodePresent` must `true`.

This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later.
If `true`, the device has a passcode. This key doesn't apply to User-Enrolled devices. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later.
If `true`, the user's passcode is compliant with all requirements on the device, including Exchange and other accounts. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later.
If `true`, the user's passcode is compliant with requirements from profiles. This key doesn't apply to User-Enrolled devices. This value is available in iOS 4 and later, and tvOS 6 and later.
The user preference for the number of seconds before a locked screen requires the device passcode to unlock it. This value is only available for Shared iPad.

The enforced value for the number of seconds before a locked screen requires the device passcode to unlock it. If a device has a passcode, changing `PasscodeLockGracePeriod` to a larger value doesn't take effect until the user logs out or removes the passcode. This value is only available for Shared iPad.
The number of seconds before a device goes to sleep after being idle. This value is only available on Shared iPad in iOS 17 and later.
If `true`, the device has enabled FileVault full disk encryption (FDE). This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later.
If `true`, FileVault FDE has a personal recovery key. This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later.
If `true`, FileVault FDE has an institutional recovery key. This value is available in macOS 10.9 and later.

If the FileVault personal recovery key has enabled escrow with a recovery key, this value contains the key. The certificate from the `FDERecoveryKeyEscrow` profile encrypts the key and wraps it as CMS data. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later.

If the FileVault personal recovery key has enabled escrow with a recovery key, this value is the device serial number. This is the value that displays to the user at the EFI Login Window as part of the help message if they enter their password incorrectly three times. The server also uses this value as an index when saving the device personal recovery key. This replaces the `recordNumber` that the server returned in the previous escrow mechanism. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later.
If `true`, System Integrity Protection (SIP) is active on the device. This value is available in macOS 10.12 and later.
A dictionary that contains the firewall settings. This value is available in macOS 10.12 and later.
If `true`, the firewall is on.
If `true`, the firewall blocks all incoming connections.
If true, stealth mode is active for the firewall.
An array of dictionaries that describes the allowed applications.
A dictionary that describes the allowed apps.
If `true`, the app is an allowed app.
The app's bundle identifier.
The app's display name if it's determinable from the `BundleID`.
If `true`, logging is enabled.
The type of logging emitted by the firewall.
A dictionary that contains the status of the EFI firmware password. This value is available in macOS 10.13 and later.
If `true`, the device has an EFI firmware password.

If `true`, a firmware password change is pending. A device restart is necessary for this change to take effect. Until then, additional attempts to change the password fail.

> Note:
> If `true`, the other values show the current state of the device, not the state after a restart.
If `true`, enable ROMs.
A dictionary that contains the status of the device's MDM enrollment.
If `true`, the device enrolled in MDM through the Device Enrollment Program (DEP). This value is available in macOS 10.13.2 and later.
If `true`, the enrollment was user-approved. If `false`, the device may reject certain security-sensitive payloads or commands. This value is available in macOS 10.13.2 and later.
If `true`, the device is user-enrolled. This value is available in iOS 13 and later, and macOS 10.15 and later.
If `true`, the type of enrollment allows the MDM to manage Activation Lock for this device. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later.
A dictionary that contains the device's Secure Boot settings. This value is available in macOS 10.15 and later.
The security level for the bootable operating system versions.
The device's external boot level, which indicates whether it allows booting from an external device, disallows it, or doesn't support it.

Reports which security features the user disables in `recoveryOS`. This property is only present for a Mac with Apple silicon when `SecureBootLevel` is `medium`.

Available in iOS 11 and later.
If 'true', allows any signed version of trusted system software from Apple to run.
If 'true', the user has control over kernel extensions.
If 'true', the MDM server controls kernel extensions and software updates.
If `true`, Remote Desktop is active on the device. This value is available in macOS 10.14.4 and later.
If `true`, the system booted using an Authenticated Root Volume. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.

This value specifies whether the Secure Enclave Processor (SEP) supports and allows secure operations to use the Bootstrap Token. The value is automatically set for devices enrolled through the Device Enrollment Program (DEP). The user can also manually set this value in the RecoveryOS.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment.

If `true`, the device can accept a Bootstrap Token from the MDM server instead of prompting for user authentication prior to installation. This only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment.

If `true`, the device can accept a Bootstrap Token from the MDM server instead of prompting for user authentication prior to enabling kernel extensions. This includes enabling kexts through the `com.apple.syspolicy.kernel-extension-policy` payload or triggering the `RestartDevice` command with `RebuildKernelCache` set to `true`. This only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response.

This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. Not available for user enrollment.
If `true`, a password is required to enter recovery (see `SetRecoveryLockCommand`). Available in macOS 11.5 and later and only on a Mac with Apple silicon.

items.title: LOM Device Request Command
items.summary: Send requests to a device using lights-out management (LOM).
items.searchKeys: ResponseList ResponseListItem DeviceRequestReturnError DeviceRequestUUID DeviceRequestSuccess RequestList RequestListItem LOMProtocolVersion SecondaryIPv6AddressList SecondaryIPv6AddressListItem PrimaryIPv6AddressList PrimaryIPv6AddressListItem DeviceDNSName DeviceRequestUUID DeviceRequestType
items.searchContent:
Send requests to a device using lights-out management (LOM). This command requires the `DeviceLockAndRemovePasscode` access right, `LightsOutManagementLOM` configuration and is available in macOS 11 and later on [supported macOS devices](https://support.apple.com/guide/deployment/lights-out-management-payload-settings-dep580cf25bc/web).

`DeviceDNSName` is the `CommonName` in the Identity issued on the client certificate from `LightsOutManagementLOM`. `LOMSetupRequestResponse` returns `PrimaryIPv6AddressList` and `SecondaryIPv6AddressList` after a successful deployment of Lights Out management configuration payload and subsequent `LOMSetupRequestCommand`. An array of dictionaries that describes the status of each request. A dictionary that describes a response list item. If present, a description of the error for a failed request. The unique identifier of the request for this response list item. If `true`, the request was successful. An array of requests to perform. A dictionary that contains a requested action to perform on a device using lights-out management (LOM). The LOM protocol version that the device supports. Provide the same value that `LOMProtocolVersion` receives in the `LOMSetupRequestResponse`. An array that contains the IPv6 addresses for secondary LOM-compatible Ethernet interfaces for the device. An array that contains the IPv6 addresses for primary LOM-compatible Ethernet interfaces for the device. The DNS name of the device. This should match the `dNSName` in `SubjectAltName` or an equivalent in a PKCS12 identity. The unique identifier of the request. The requested action to perform on the device. Used to send LOM requests ("PowerON", "PowerOFF", "Reset") to LOM Controller which then forwards the request to LOM Devices.

The requested action to perform on the device.
The unique identifier of the request.
The DNS name of the device. This should match the `dNSName` in `SubjectAltName` or an equivalent in a PKCS12 identity.
An array that contains the IPv6 addresses for primary LOM-compatible Ethernet interfaces for the device.
An array that contains the IPv6 addresses for secondary LOM-compatible Ethernet interfaces for the device.
The LOM protocol version that the device supports. Provide the same value that `LOMProtocolVersion` receives in the `LOMSetupRequestResponse`.

responsekeys.content: An array of dictionaries that describes the status of each request.

subkeys.content: A dictionary that describes a response list item.
If `true`, the request was successful.
The unique identifier of the request for this response list item.
If present, a description of the error for a failed request.

notes.content:
This command requires the `DeviceLockAndRemovePasscode` access right, `LightsOutManagementLOM` configuration and is available in macOS 11 and later on [supported macOS devices](https://support.apple.com/guide/deployment/lights-out-management-payload-settings-dep580cf25bc/web).

`DeviceDNSName` is the `CommonName` in the Identity issued on the client certificate from `LightsOutManagementLOM`. `LOMSetupRequestResponse` returns `PrimaryIPv6AddressList` and `SecondaryIPv6AddressList` after a successful deployment of Lights Out management configuration payload and subsequent `LOMSetupRequestCommand`.

items.title: Managed Application Attributes Command
items.summary: Query attributes in managed apps on a device.
items.searchKeys: ApplicationAttributes ApplicationAttributesItem Attributes Lockable Hideable CellularSliceUUID TapToPayScreenLock Removable AssociatedDomainsEnableDirectDownloads AssociatedDomains AssociatedDomain RelayUUID DNSProxyUUID ContentFilterUUID VPNUUID Identifier Identifiers IdentifiersItem
items.searchContent:
Query attributes in managed apps on a device. This command allows the server to get attributes of managed apps.

The response doesn't include apps that Declarative Device Management is managing.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of app attribute items. A dictionary that contains a managed app attributes item. The app's attributes. If `false`, the system prevents the user from locking the app. This also prevents the user from hiding the app. If `false`, the system prevents the user from hiding the app. It doesn't affect the user's ability to leave it in the App Library, while removing it from the Home Screen. The data network name (DNN) or app category. For DNN, the value is `DNN:name`, where `name` is the carrier-provided DNN name. For app category, the value is `AppCategory:category`, where `category` is a carrier-provided string like "Enterprise1".

Available in iOS 17 and later. If `true`, Tap to Pay on iPhone requires users to use Face ID or a passcode to unlock their device after every transaction that requires a customer's card PIN. If `false`, the user can configure this setting on their device.

Available in iOS 16.4 and later. If `false`, this app isn't removable while it's a managed app. This value is available in iOS 14 and later. If `true`, perform claimed site association verification directly at the domain instead of on Apple's servers. Only set this to `true` for domains that can't access the internet. This value is available in iOS 14 and later. This app's associated domains. This value is available in iOS 13 and later. The relay UUID for this app. The DNS Proxy UUID assigned to this app.

Available in iOS 16 and later. The content Filter UUID assigned to this app.

Available in iOS 16 and later. A per-app VPN unique identifier for this app. The app's bundle identifier.

> Note:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier is the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. The bundle identifiers of the managed apps.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.

If `false`, this app isn't removable while it's a managed app. This value is available in iOS 14 and later.

If `true`, Tap to Pay on iPhone requires users to use Face ID or a passcode to unlock their device after every transaction that requires a customer's card PIN. If `false`, the user can configure this setting on their device.

Available in iOS 16.4 and later.

The data network name (DNN) or app category. For DNN, the value is `DNN:name`, where `name` is the carrier-provided DNN name. For app category, the value is `AppCategory:category`, where `category` is a carrier-provided string like "Enterprise1".

Available in iOS 17 and later.
If `false`, the system prevents the user from hiding the app. It doesn't affect the user's ability to leave it in the App Library, while removing it from the Home Screen.

items.title: Install Media Command
items.summary: Install a book on a device.
items.searchKeys: RejectionReason State MediaType PersistentID MediaURL iTunesStoreID Title Author Version Kind PersistentID MediaType MediaURL iTunesStoreID
items.searchContent:
Install a book on a device. The request must contain either the `iTunesStoreID` or `MediaURL`. The `MediaURL` must lead to a PDF file, an EPUB file in `gzip` format, or an iBooks Author document in `gzip` format. Books that MDM has installed become managed books.

Use Volume Purchase Program (VPP) Licensing to obtain books from the Book Store. Books from the Book Store require that the device has enabled App Store. These books undergo backup, sync with iTunes, and remain on the device after removal of the MDM profile.

Books that aren't from the Book Store don't require that the device has enabled App Store. These books don't undergo backup, don't sync with iTunes, and don't remain on the device after removal of the MDM profile.

If the book already exists, this command updates the book and makes it visible to the MDM server. The user doesn't receive a prompt for a book installation or update unless they need to log in to complete a Book Store transaction.

If you install a book from the Book Store with the same `iTunesStoreID` as an existing managed book, the new book replaces the existing one.

If you install a book that isn't from the Book Store with the same `PersistentID` as an existing book that also isn't from the Book Store, the new book replaces the existing one.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The reason, if installation fails, which is one of the following values:

- `CouldNotVerifyITunesStoreID`: The `iTunesStoreID` is invalid.
- `PurchaseNotFound`: The Volume Purchase Program (VPP) license isn't in the user's history.
- `AppStoreDisabled`: App Store isn't available on the device.
- `WrongMediaType`: The media type is invalid. The only valid type is `Book`.
- `DownloadInvalid`: The URL doesn't lead to a valid book.
- `EnterpriseBooksNotSupportedInMultiUser`: Multiuser mode doesn't support enterprise books. The installation state of this book. The `Failed` and `Unknown` states are transient and the device only reports them once. Books from the Book Store report their state as `Installed` instead of `Managed`. The media type, which can only be `Book`. The book's persistent identifier, if present in the command. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The URL to retrieve the book, if present in the command. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The book's iTunes Store identifier, if present in the command. The book's title. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The name of the book's author. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The book's version number. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The kind of the media, which can be one of the following values:

- `pdf`: A PDF file
- `epub`: An EPUB file in `gzip` format.
- `ibooks`: An iBooks Author file in `gzip` format.

If you omit this value, its value is the file extension in the URL. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The book's persistent identifier in reverse-DNS form; for example, `com.acme.manuals.training`. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The media type, which can only be `Book`. The URL to retrieve the book. This value is available in iOS 8 and later. The book's iTunes Store identifier. This command allows the server to install a book on a device. If the book is already being managed, this command will update the book.

The name of the book's author. This value is available in iOS 8 and later.
The book's title. This value is available in iOS 8 and later.

The URL to retrieve the book, if present in the command. This value is available in iOS 8 and later.
The book's persistent identifier, if present in the command. This value is available in iOS 8 and later.
The media type, which can only be `Book`.

The installation state of this book. The `Failed` and `Unknown` states are transient and the device only reports them once. Books from the Book Store report their state as `Installed` instead of `Managed`.

The reason, if installation fails, which is one of the following values:

- `CouldNotVerifyITunesStoreID`: The `iTunesStoreID` is invalid.
- `PurchaseNotFound`: The Volume Purchase Program (VPP) license isn't in the user's history.
- `AppStoreDisabled`: App Store isn't available on the device.
- `WrongMediaType`: The media type is invalid. The only valid type is `Book`.
- `DownloadInvalid`: The URL doesn't lead to a valid book.
- `EnterpriseBooksNotSupportedInMultiUser`: Multiuser mode doesn't support enterprise books.

notes.content:
The request must contain either the `iTunesStoreID` or `MediaURL`. The `MediaURL` must lead to a PDF file, an EPUB file in `gzip` format, or an iBooks Author document in `gzip` format. Books that MDM has installed become managed books.

Use Volume Purchase Program (VPP) Licensing to obtain books from the Book Store. Books from the Book Store require that the device has enabled App Store. These books undergo backup, sync with iTunes, and remain on the device after removal of the MDM profile.

Books that aren't from the Book Store don't require that the device has enabled App Store. These books don't undergo backup, don't sync with iTunes, and don't remain on the device after removal of the MDM profile.

If the book already exists, this command updates the book and makes it visible to the MDM server. The user doesn't receive a prompt for a book installation or update unless they need to log in to complete a Book Store transaction.

If you install a book from the Book Store with the same `iTunesStoreID` as an existing managed book, the new book replaces the existing one.

If you install a book that isn't from the Book Store with the same `PersistentID` as an existing book that also isn't from the Book Store, the new book replaces the existing one.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Request Mirroring Command
items.summary: Prompt the user to share their screen using AirPlay Mirroring.
items.searchKeys: MirroringResult Password ScanTime DestinationDeviceID DestinationName
items.searchContent:
Prompt the user to share their screen using AirPlay Mirroring. Provide either the `DestinationName` or the `DestinationDeviceID`. If you provide both values, MDM uses `DestinationDeviceID`.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The result of the request. One of these values:

- `Prompting`: The user is receiving a prompt to share their screen.
- `DestinationNotFound`: The device is unable to reach the destination.
- `Cancelled`: The user canceled the request.
- `Unknown`: An unknown error occurred. The screen-sharing password that the device uses when connecting to the destination. The number of seconds, from `10` to `300`, for the device to spend searching for the destination. The default value is `30`. The hardware address of the AirPlay Mirroring destination that identifies the device, in the format `xx:xx:xx:xx:xx`. This value isn't case-sensitive. Not available for Apple TV devices running tvOS 18 or later, use `DestinationName` instead. The name of the AirPlay Mirroring destination. This command prompts the user to share their screen using AirPlay Mirroring.

declaration.title: Request Mirroring Command
declaration.description: Prompt the user to share their screen using AirPlay Mirroring.

payload.content: This command prompts the user to share their screen using AirPlay Mirroring.

payloadkeys.content: The name of the AirPlay Mirroring destination.

The hardware address of the AirPlay Mirroring destination that identifies the device, in the format `xx:xx:xx:xx:xx`. This value isn't case-sensitive. Not available for Apple TV devices running tvOS 18 or later, use `DestinationName` instead.
The number of seconds, from `10` to `300`, for the device to spend searching for the destination. The default value is `30`.
The screen-sharing password that the device uses when connecting to the destination.

responsekeys.content:
The result of the request. One of these values:

- `Prompting`: The user is receiving a prompt to share their screen.
- `DestinationNotFound`: The device is unable to reach the destination.
- `Cancelled`: The user canceled the request.
- `Unknown`: An unknown error occurred.

notes.content:
Provide either the `DestinationName` or the `DestinationDeviceID`. If you provide both values, MDM uses `DestinationDeviceID`.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Clear Passcode Command
items.summary: Remove the passcode from a device.
items.searchKeys: UnlockToken
items.searchContent:
Remove the passcode from a device. Clearing the passcode in iOS 16 no longer adds the passcode to the history of passcodes. Therefore, the user can reuse the cleared passcode even when the `Passcode` payload has the `pinHistory` key set.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The unlock token value that the device provides in its `TokenUpdateMessage` check-in message. This command allows the server to clear the passcode on the device. This command requires the Device Lock and Passcode Removal right.

declaration.description: Remove the passcode from a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to clear the passcode on the device. This command requires the Device Lock and Passcode Removal right.

payloadkeys.content: The unlock token value that the device provides in its `TokenUpdateMessage` check-in message.

notes.content:
Clearing the passcode in iOS 16 no longer adds the passcode to the history of passcodes. Therefore, the user can reuse the cleared passcode even when the `Passcode` payload has the `pinHistory` key set.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Set Firmware Password Command
items.summary: Change or clear the firmware password on a device.
items.searchKeys: SetFirmwarePassword PasswordChanged AllowOroms NewPassword CurrentPassword
items.searchContent:
Change or clear the firmware password on a device. This command has a throttle interval to prevent executing it more frequently than every 30 seconds. Requests that occur within the throttle interval return an error.

> Important:
> There's no way to set or clear a firmware password in MDM without knowing the current password, unless the server provides a way to prompt the administrator for the current password. Contact AppleCare service and support if the current password is unknown.

After processing the command, the device restarts so that the new firmware password takes effect. This command returns an error and fails if a firmware password is already pending.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

This command isn't supported on a Mac with Apple silicon. A dictionary containing the results of the command. If `true`, the password change succeeded. If `true`, enable ROMs. The new firmware password. Set to an empty string to clear the password. The characters in this value must consist of low-ASCII, printable characters (`0x20` through `0x7E`) to ensure that all characters are enterable on the EFI login screen. The current password, which you must set if the device has a firmware password. Changes or clears the firmware password for the device. Requires the "Device lock and passcode removal right". This command is not available on a Mac with Apple silicon.

declaration.title: Set Firmware Password Command
declaration.description: Change or clear the firmware password on a device.

payload.content: Changes or clears the firmware password for the device. Requires the "Device lock and passcode removal right". This command is not available on a Mac with Apple silicon.

payloadkeys.content: The current password, which you must set if the device has a firmware password.

The new firmware password. Set to an empty string to clear the password. The characters in this value must consist of low-ASCII, printable characters (`0x20` through `0x7E`) to ensure that all characters are enterable on the EFI login screen.
If `true`, enable ROMs.

responsekeys.content: A dictionary containing the results of the command.

subkeys.content: If `true`, the password change succeeded.

notes.content:
This command has a throttle interval to prevent executing it more frequently than every 30 seconds. Requests that occur within the throttle interval return an error.

> Important:
> There's no way to set or clear a firmware password in MDM without knowing the current password, unless the server provides a way to prompt the administrator for the current password. Contact AppleCare service and support if the current password is unknown.

After processing the command, the device restarts so that the new firmware password takes effect. This command returns an error and fails if a firmware password is already pending.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

This command isn't supported on a Mac with Apple silicon.

items.title: Set Recovery Lock Command
items.summary: Set or clear the Recovery Lock password.
items.searchKeys: NewPassword CurrentPassword
items.searchContent:
Set or clear the Recovery Lock password. This command sets, or clears, a password on booting to recoveryOS. When the device unenrolls MDM the system removes the recovery password.

This command is only available on a Mac with Apple silicon. The new password for Recovery Lock. Set as an empty string to clear the Recovery Lock password. If the device has a Recovery Lock password set, the system requires the current password. Sets or clears the recovery lock password (Apple Silicon devices only). Requires the "Device lock and passcode removal right".

The new password for Recovery Lock. Set as an empty string to clear the Recovery Lock password.

This command sets, or clears, a password on booting to recoveryOS. When the device unenrolls MDM the system removes the recovery password.

This command is only available on a Mac with Apple silicon.

items.title: Disable Remote Desktop Command
items.summary: Disable Remote Desktop on a device.
items.searchContent:
Disable Remote Desktop on a device. This command disables Remote Desktop on the device, and prevents any further remote event processing. It removes any `PostEvent` Transparency Consent and Control (TCC) ability, unless the device already has an installed TCC configuration profile with that ability enabled.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. Disable Remote Desktop.

declaration.description: Disable Remote Desktop on a device.

payload.content: Disable Remote Desktop.

notes.content:
This command disables Remote Desktop on the device, and prevents any further remote event processing. It removes any `PostEvent` Transparency Consent and Control (TCC) ability, unless the device already has an installed TCC configuration profile with that ability enabled.

items.title: Enable Remote Desktop Command
items.summary: Enable Remote Desktop on a device.

Enable Remote Desktop on a device. This command enables the following capabilities on the device:

- Remote Desktop with the All Users access
- The ability to receive remote events
- The Observe, Control, and Show being Observed options

All other options remain unchanged.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. Enable Remote Desktop.

items.title: Rotate FileVault Key Command
items.summary: Change the FileVault primary password on a device.
items.searchKeys: RotateResult EncryptedNewRecoveryKey ReplyEncryptionCertificate NewCertificate FileVaultUnlock PrivateKeyExportPassword PrivateKeyExport Password KeyType
items.searchTitles: Rotate FileVault Key Command  PrivateKeyExportPassword PrivateKeyExport Password
items.searchContent:
Change the FileVault primary password on a device. Change the FileVault password periodically to mitigate the security risk of deployed devices.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The result of rotating the personal recovery key. A new personal recovery key that is encrypted using a `ReplyEncryptionCertificate` as a CMS-compliant envelope. A DER-encoded certificate for encrypting the new personal recovery key in a wrapper conforming to the IETF Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) standard. A DER-encoded certificate for creating a new institutional recovery key, which the system requires if `KeyType` is `institutional`. A dictionary that contains FileVault unlock options. The password for `PrivateKeyExport`. Either `Password` or both `PrivateKeyExport` and `PrivateKeyExportPassword` must be present. The system ignores this key on APFS volumes. The data for a .p12 export of the private key for the current institutional recovery key, which requires that `KeyType` is `institutional`. The system ignores this key on APFS volumes. A FileVault user's password, or if using a CoreStorage volume, the personal recovery key. The type of FileVault key you want to change the password for. Set this value to `personal` and set a value for `Password` in the `FileVaultUnlock` dictionary to enable unlocking a device with a password. Set this value to `institutional` and set values for `PrivateKeyExport` and `PrivateKeyExportPassword` in the `FileVaultUnlock` dictionary. This command allows for changing a device's FileVaultMaster password.

declaration.title: Rotate FileVault Key Command
declaration.description: Change the FileVault primary password on a device.

payload.content: This command allows for changing a device's FileVaultMaster password.

Set this value to `personal` and set a value for `Password` in the `FileVaultUnlock` dictionary to enable unlocking a device with a password. Set this value to `institutional` and set values for `PrivateKeyExport` and `PrivateKeyExportPassword` in the `FileVaultUnlock` dictionary.
A dictionary that contains FileVault unlock options.

subkeys.title: Password
subkeys.content: A FileVault user's password, or if using a CoreStorage volume, the personal recovery key.

subkeys.title: PrivateKeyExport
subkeys.content: The data for a .p12 export of the private key for the current institutional recovery key, which requires that `KeyType` is `institutional`. The system ignores this key on APFS volumes.

subkeys.title: PrivateKeyExportPassword
subkeys.content: The password for `PrivateKeyExport`. Either `Password` or both `PrivateKeyExport` and `PrivateKeyExportPassword` must be present. The system ignores this key on APFS volumes.

payloadkeys.content: A DER-encoded certificate for creating a new institutional recovery key, which the system requires if `KeyType` is `institutional`.
A DER-encoded certificate for encrypting the new personal recovery key in a wrapper conforming to the IETF Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) standard.

responsekeys.content: The result of rotating the personal recovery key.

subkeys.content: A new personal recovery key that is encrypted using a `ReplyEncryptionCertificate` as a CMS-compliant envelope.

notes.content:
Change the FileVault password periodically to mitigate the security risk of deployed devices.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Set Auto Admin Password Command
items.summary: Update the local administrator account password.
items.searchKeys: passwordHash GUID
items.searchContent:
Update the local administrator account password. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The precreated salted SHA-512 PBKDF2 password hash for the account.

Create this hash on the server using the CommonCrypto libraries, or equivalent, as a salted SHA-512 PBKDF2 dictionary that contains these elements:

- `entropy`: The derived key from the password hash; for example, from `CCKeyDerivationPBKDF()`
- `salt`: The 32-byte randomized salt; for example, from `CCRandomCopyBytes()`
- `iterations:` The number of iterations; for example, from `CCCalibratePBKDF()` using a minimum hash time of 100 milliseconds, or if unknown, a number in the range of 20,000 to 40,000 iterations

Place the dictionary that contains these elements into an outer dictionary with the key `SALTED-SHA512-PBKDF2`. Convert this dictionary to binary data before setting it as the value for `passwordHash`. The unique identifier of the local administrator account. If this value doesn't match the GUID of an administrator account that MDM created during Device Enrollment Program (DEP) enrollment, the command returns an error. Allows changing the password of a local admin account that was created by Setup Assistant during DEP enrollment via the AccountConfiguration command.

declaration.title: Set Auto Admin Password Command
declaration.description: Update the local administrator account password.

payload.content: Allows changing the password of a local admin account that was created by Setup Assistant during DEP enrollment via the AccountConfiguration command.

payloadkeys.content: The unique identifier of the local administrator account. If this value doesn't match the GUID of an administrator account that MDM created during Device Enrollment Program (DEP) enrollment, the command returns an error.

The precreated salted SHA-512 PBKDF2 password hash for the account.

Create this hash on the server using the CommonCrypto libraries, or equivalent, as a salted SHA-512 PBKDF2 dictionary that contains these elements:

- `entropy`: The derived key from the password hash; for example, from `CCKeyDerivationPBKDF()`
- `salt`: The 32-byte randomized salt; for example, from `CCRandomCopyBytes()`
- `iterations:` The number of iterations; for example, from `CCCalibratePBKDF()` using a minimum hash time of 100 milliseconds, or if unknown, a number in the range of 20,000 to 40,000 iterations

Place the dictionary that contains these elements into an outer dictionary with the key `SALTED-SHA512-PBKDF2`. Convert this dictionary to binary data before setting it as the value for `passwordHash`.

notes.content: Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.
