items.searchContent:
Configure settings on a device. Users may be able to change the settings later if a profile isn't set to restrict such changes.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. A dictionary that describes the results of configuring settings. The app identifier to which this error applies.

> Note:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier is the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. An array of dictionaries that describes any errors that occurred. A dictionary that describes an error chain item. A dictionary that contains additional details about the error. The status of the setting, which is one of the following values:

- `Acknowledged`: The device processed the command successfully.
- `Error`: An error occurred. See the `ErrorChain` for more details. An array of dictionaries that contains the settings. A dictionary that contains accessibility settings. Available in iOS 16 and later. If `true`, the system enables grayscale display. If `true`, the system enables zoom. If `true`, the system enables voiceover. If `true`, the system enables touch accommodations. The accessibility text size apps that support dynamic text use. `0` is the smallest value, and `11` is the largest available. If `true`, the system enables reduced transparency. If `true`, the system enables reduced motion. If `true`, the system enables increase contrast. If `true`, the system enables bold text. Sets various accessibility settings. The system allows only keys with explicitly provided values. A dictionary that contains software update settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 14.5 and later. This value defines how the system presents software updates to the user. When there's more than one available update for the user, the system behaves as follows:

- `0`: Presents both options to the user.
- `1`: Presents the lower numbered (oldest) software update version.
- `2`: Presents only the highest numbered (most recent) release available for the device.

This value has no effect when there's only one available update; the system shows the single available update to the user regardless of the value of this setting.

Available in iOS 14.5 and later. A string that represents the type of updates that should appear in the Software Update pane in Settings. Supervised only. A dictionary that contains time zone settings. This setting is available only on supervised devices and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 14 and later, tvOS 14 and later, and visionOS 2 and later. The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) time zone database name.

If the `forceAutomaticDateAndTime` restriction is set in `Restrictions`, this setting fails with an error. Otherwise, setting this value disables automatic time zone logic. The user is still be able to change the time zone; for example, by turning automatic date and time back on. The intention is to allow setting the time zone when automatic determination isn't be available, such as when Location Services are off. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains password lock grace period settings. This setting is available only for Shared iPad in iOS 9.3.2 and later.
This key is deprecated. Use 'PasscodeLockGracePeriod' in SettingsCommand.Command.Settings.SharedDeviceConfiguration.PasscodePolicy instead. The number of seconds before a locked screen requires the user to enter the device passcode to unlock it. The minimum value is `0` seconds and the maximum value is `14400` seconds.

If a device has a passcode, a change to a larger value doesn't take effect until the user logs out or removes the passcode. For this reason, it's better to set this value before the user sets a passcode.

If the value is less than one of the known values, the device uses the next lowest value. For example a value of 299 results in an effective setting of 60.

This setting won't take effect if `TemporarySessionOnly` is `true` because there's no passcode for a temporary session. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains settings for sharing app analytics. This setting is available only for Shared iPad in iOS 9.3.2 and later. If `true`, enable sharing app analytics with app developers. If `false`, disable sharing app analytics. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains diagnostic submission settings. This setting is available only for Shared iPad in iOS 9.3 and later. If `true`, enables diagnostic submission. If `false`, disables diagnostic submission. The string that defines this setting type. A dictionary that contains shared device configuration settings. This setting is available only for Shared iPad in iOS 13.4 and later. A dictionary that contains passcode policies. The number of seconds before a device goes to sleep after being idle. The minimum value for this setting is `120` seconds. The number of seconds before a locked screen requires the user to enter the device passcode to unlock it. The minimum value is `0` seconds and the maximum value is `14400` seconds.

If a device has a passcode, a change to a larger value doesn't take effect until the user logs out or removes the passcode. For this reason, it's better to set this value before the user sets a passcode.

If the value is less than one of the known values, the device uses the next lowest value. For example a value of 299 results in an effective setting of 60.

This setting won't take effect if `TemporarySessionOnly` is `true` because there's no passcode for a temporary session. If enabled, the Shared iPad device enters Setup Assistant after the user triggers a login. The MDM server has a chance to configure the device and user. After configuration, the server needs to send a `User-Configured-Command` command to the user channel to unblock the login. This feature requires the device to have network access during the login process.

Available in iOS 17 and later. If `true`, the device stops at the Setup Assistant pane after user login. The user can't use the device until it receives a `User-Configured-Command` command. If `true`, the system picks the system language and locale automatically for the new Shared iPad user.

Available in iOS 16.2 and later. A grace period (in days) for Shared iPad online authentication. The Shared iPad only verifies the user's passcode locally during login for users that already exist on the device. However, the system requires an online authentication (against Apple's identity server) after the number of days specified by this setting.

Setting this value to 0 enforces online authentication every time.

Available in iOS 16 and later. A list of domains that the Shared iPad login screen displays. The user can pick a domain from the list to complete their Managed Apple Account.

If this list contains more than 3 domains, the system picks 3 at random for display. Available in iOS 16 and later. If `true`, the user only sees the Guest Welcome pane and can only log in as a guest user.

If `false`, the user can sign in with a Managed Apple Account (the existing behavior).

Available in iOS 14.5 and later. The timeout, in seconds, for the temporary session. The temporary session logs out automatically after the specified period of inactivity. The minimum value is 30 seconds. Setting this value to `0` removes the timeout.

Available in iOS 14.5 and later. The timeout, in seconds, for the user session. The user session logs out automatically after the specified period of inactivity. The minimum value is 30 seconds. Setting this value to `0` removes the timeout.

Available in iOS 14.5 and later. The expected number of users. If this value is greater than the value for the maximum possible number of users that the device supports, the MDM server uses that value instead. The quota size, in megabytes (MB), for each user on the shared device, or if the quota size is too small, the minimum quota size. Available to Temporary Sessions Only guest users on iOS 17+. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains settings for maximum resident users. Apple deprecated this setting in iOS 13.4. Use 'SharedDeviceConfiguration` instead. This setting is available only for Shared iPad. The maximum number of users that can use the device. If this value is greater than the value for the maximum possible number of users that the device supports, the MDM server uses that value instead.

This setting requires that the device is in the `AwaitingConfiguration` phase before it receives the `DeviceConfiguredCommand` message.

When a device reaches the maximum number of resident users and a new user tries to sign in, the MDM server removes a synchronized user to make space for the new user. If there are no synchronized users, the new user sign-in fails. A synchronized user is a user that has completed syncing their data. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains settings related to the MDM protocol. This setting doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and visionOS 2 and later. A dictionary of MDM options. If `true`, the device automatically reboots while locked after several days of inactivity. This is set to `false` by default when a supervised device enrolls. If `true`, warn the user that they need to reboot into RecoveryOS and allow the MDM server to use the Bootstrap Token for authentication for certain sensitive operations; for example, enabling kernel extensions or installing certain types of software updates. Set this value to `false` if your MDM server doesn't need to perform these operations. The value provided here overrides the value specified in MDM, and only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response. This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. If `true`, the server supports the Bootstrap Token commands. If `true`, a supervised device registers itself with Activation Lock when the user enables Find My. This setting is available for supervised devices in iOS 7 and later, and macOS 10.15 and later. The string that defines this setting type. A dictionary that contains default application bundle identifiers for each default application type that can be set. The bundle identifier of the app that the system sets as the default messaging app. This app must be an eligible messaging app. The bundle identifier of the app that the system sets as the default calling app. This app must be an eligible calling app. The bundle identifier of the app the system sets as the default web browser. This app must be an eligible web browser for the region of the device. A dictionary that contains settings about the organization operating the MDM server. This setting supports user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later. A dictionary that contains information about the organization operating the MDM server. Omit this setting to remove existing information. A unique identifier for the various services a single organization manages. The organization's support email address. The organization's phone number. The organization's address. Use the LF character (`&#10`) to insert line breaks. A shorter version of `OrganizationName`, preferably a single word or abbreviation, suitable for display to the user in places where a very short name is necessary. A string that describes the organization operating the MDM server for display to the user during certain operations, such as purchasing or installing apps. The string that defines this setting type. A dictionary that contains hostname settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment, and is available in macOS 10.11 and later. The hostname for the device. The string that defines this setting type. A dictionary that contains device name settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment, and is available only on supervised devices. Available in iOS 5 and later, macOS 10.10 and later, and visionOS 2 and later. The device's name. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains the attributes to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing attributes. This setting supports user enrollment, is available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. This setting fails for apps that Declarative Device Management manages. A dictionary that contains the attributes to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing attributes. This setting is available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. If `false`, the system prevents the user from locking the app. This also prevents the user from hiding the app. If `false`, the system prevents the user from hiding the app. It doesn't affect the user's ability to leave it in the App Library, while removing it from the Home Screen. The data network name (DNN) or app category. For DNN, the value is `DNN:name`, where `name` is the carrier-provided DNN name. For app category, the value is `AppCategory:category`, where `category` is a carrier-provided string like "Enterprise1"`.`

Available in iOS 17 and later. If true, the system require Tap to Pay on iPhone users to use Face ID or a passcode to unlock their device after every transaction that requires a customer's card PIN. If `false`, the user can configure this setting on their device.

Available in iOS 16.4 and later. If `false`, this app isn't removable while it's managed. Available in iOS 14 and later, and tvOS 14 and later. If `true`, perform claimed site association verification directly at the domain, instead of on Apple's servers. Only set this to `true` for domains that can't access the internet. Available in iOS 14 and later. An array that contains the associated domains to add to this app. Available in iOS 13 and later. The relay UUID for this app. Available in iOS 17 and later. The DNS proxy UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later. The content filter UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later. A per-app VPN unique identifier for this app. Available in iOS 7 and later. The bundle identifier of the app.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains the configurations to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing configurations. This setting requires the App Management access right, supports user enrollment, and is available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. This setting fails for apps that Declarative Device Management manages. A dictionary that contains the configurations to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing configurations. A dictionary that contains configurations. The bundle identifier of the managed app.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains Bluetooth settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, doesn't support user enrollment, and is available only on supervised devices. Available in iOS 11.3 and later, and macOS 10.13.4 and later. If `true`, enable the Bluetooth setting. If `false`, disable the Bluetooth setting. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains Personal Hotspot settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later. If `true`, enable Personal Hotspot. If `false`, disable Personal Hotspot. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains voice roaming settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later. If `true`, enable voice roaming. If `false`, disable voice roaming, which also disables data roaming. The setting is only available for certain carriers. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains data roaming settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later. If `true`, enable data roaming, which also enables voice roaming. If `false`, disable data roaming. A string that identifies this setting. A dictionary that contains wallpaper settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 8 and later. Starting in iOS 16 and iPadOS 17, when setting the wallpaper for the first time, both locations update. After that, you can set either location separately. A number that indicates where to use the wallpaper, which is one of the following values:

- `1`: Lock Screen
- `2`: Home Screen
- `3`: Both Lock and Home Screens.

  In iOS 16 and later, and iPadOS 17 and later, when you set the wallpaper for the first time, the system sets both the Lock Screen and Home Screen. After that, you can separately set each location. A Base64-encoded image in either PNG or JPG format to use for wallpaper. A string that identifies this setting. This command allows the server to set settings on the device. These settings take effect on a one-time basis. The user may still be able to change the settings at a later time. This command requires the ApplySettings right.

declaration.title: Settings Command
declaration.description: Configure settings on a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to set settings on the device. These settings take effect on a one-time basis. The user may still be able to change the settings at a later time. This command requires the ApplySettings right.

payloadkeys.content: An array of dictionaries that contains the settings.

subkeys.content: A dictionary that contains wallpaper settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 8 and later. Starting in iOS 16 and iPadOS 17, when setting the wallpaper for the first time, both locations update. After that, you can set either location separately.
A string that identifies this setting.
A Base64-encoded image in either PNG or JPG format to use for wallpaper.

A number that indicates where to use the wallpaper, which is one of the following values:

- `1`: Lock Screen
- `2`: Home Screen
- `3`: Both Lock and Home Screens.

  In iOS 16 and later, and iPadOS 17 and later, when you set the wallpaper for the first time, the system sets both the Lock Screen and Home Screen. After that, you can separately set each location.
A dictionary that contains data roaming settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
If `true`, enable data roaming, which also enables voice roaming. If `false`, disable data roaming.
A dictionary that contains voice roaming settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
If `true`, enable voice roaming. If `false`, disable voice roaming, which also disables data roaming. The setting is only available for certain carriers.
A dictionary that contains Personal Hotspot settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, and doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
If `true`, enable Personal Hotspot. If `false`, disable Personal Hotspot.

A dictionary that contains Bluetooth settings. This setting requires the Network Information access right, doesn't support user enrollment, and is available only on supervised devices. Available in iOS 11.3 and later, and macOS 10.13.4 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
If `true`, enable the Bluetooth setting. If `false`, disable the Bluetooth setting.

A dictionary that contains the configurations to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing configurations. This setting requires the App Management access right, supports user enrollment, and is available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. This setting fails for apps that Declarative Device Management manages.
A string that identifies this setting.

The bundle identifier of the managed app.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.
A dictionary that contains the configurations to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing configurations.
A dictionary that contains configurations.

A dictionary that contains the attributes to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing attributes. This setting supports user enrollment, is available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later. This setting fails for apps that Declarative Device Management manages.
A string that identifies this setting.

The bundle identifier of the app.

> Important:
> For a watchOS app, the identifier needs to be the watch's bundle identifier, which differs from the main bundle identifier for the iPhone to which the watch is paired. Obtain the watch's bundle identifier for an app with a watch bundle, in the `watchBundleId` key that's part of the Content Metadata query. For more information on this query, see `Getting App and Book Information`.
A dictionary that contains the attributes to apply to the app. Omit this setting to remove existing attributes. This setting is available in iOS 7 and later, and tvOS 10.2 and later.
A per-app VPN unique identifier for this app. Available in iOS 7 and later.
The content filter UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later.
The DNS proxy UUID for this app. Available in iOS 16 and later.
The relay UUID for this app. Available in iOS 17 and later.
An array that contains the associated domains to add to this app. Available in iOS 13 and later.

If `true`, perform claimed site association verification directly at the domain, instead of on Apple's servers. Only set this to `true` for domains that can't access the internet. Available in iOS 14 and later.
If `false`, this app isn't removable while it's managed. Available in iOS 14 and later, and tvOS 14 and later.

If true, the system require Tap to Pay on iPhone users to use Face ID or a passcode to unlock their device after every transaction that requires a customer's card PIN. If `false`, the user can configure this setting on their device.

Available in iOS 16.4 and later.

The data network name (DNN) or app category. For DNN, the value is `DNN:name`, where `name` is the carrier-provided DNN name. For app category, the value is `AppCategory:category`, where `category` is a carrier-provided string like "Enterprise1"`.`

Available in iOS 17 and later.
If `false`, the system prevents the user from hiding the app. It doesn't affect the user's ability to leave it in the App Library, while removing it from the Home Screen.
If `false`, the system prevents the user from locking the app. This also prevents the user from hiding the app.

A dictionary that contains device name settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment, and is available only on supervised devices. Available in iOS 5 and later, macOS 10.10 and later, and visionOS 2 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
The device's name.
A dictionary that contains hostname settings. This setting doesn't support user enrollment, and is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
The string that defines this setting type.
The hostname for the device.
A dictionary that contains settings about the organization operating the MDM server. This setting supports user enrollment. Available in iOS 5 and later.
The string that defines this setting type.
A dictionary that contains information about the organization operating the MDM server. Omit this setting to remove existing information.
A string that describes the organization operating the MDM server for display to the user during certain operations, such as purchasing or installing apps.
A shorter version of `OrganizationName`, preferably a single word or abbreviation, suitable for display to the user in places where a very short name is necessary.
The organization's address. Use the LF character (`&#10`) to insert line breaks.
The organization's phone number.
The organization's support email address.
A unique identifier for the various services a single organization manages.
A dictionary that contains default application bundle identifiers for each default application type that can be set.
The bundle identifier of the app the system sets as the default web browser. This app must be an eligible web browser for the region of the device.
The bundle identifier of the app that the system sets as the default calling app. This app must be an eligible calling app.
The bundle identifier of the app that the system sets as the default messaging app. This app must be an eligible messaging app.
A dictionary that contains settings related to the MDM protocol. This setting doesn't support user enrollment. Available in iOS 7 and later, macOS 10.15 and later, and visionOS 2 and later.
The string that defines this setting type.
A dictionary of MDM options.
If `true`, a supervised device registers itself with Activation Lock when the user enables Find My. This setting is available for supervised devices in iOS 7 and later, and macOS 10.15 and later.
If `true`, the server supports the Bootstrap Token commands.

If `true`, warn the user that they need to reboot into RecoveryOS and allow the MDM server to use the Bootstrap Token for authentication for certain sensitive operations; for example, enabling kernel extensions or installing certain types of software updates. Set this value to `false` if your MDM server doesn't need to perform these operations. The value provided here overrides the value specified in MDM, and only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response. This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later.
If `true`, the device automatically reboots while locked after several days of inactivity. This is set to `false` by default when a supervised device enrolls.
A dictionary that contains settings for maximum resident users. Apple deprecated this setting in iOS 13.4. Use 'SharedDeviceConfiguration` instead. This setting is available only for Shared iPad.
A string that identifies this setting.

The maximum number of users that can use the device. If this value is greater than the value for the maximum possible number of users that the device supports, the MDM server uses that value instead.

This setting requires that the device is in the `AwaitingConfiguration` phase before it receives the `DeviceConfiguredCommand` message.

When a device reaches the maximum number of resident users and a new user tries to sign in, the MDM server removes a synchronized user to make space for the new user. If there are no synchronized users, the new user sign-in fails. A synchronized user is a user that has completed syncing their data.
A dictionary that contains shared device configuration settings. This setting is available only for Shared iPad in iOS 13.4 and later.
A string that identifies this setting.
The quota size, in megabytes (MB), for each user on the shared device, or if the quota size is too small, the minimum quota size. Available to Temporary Sessions Only guest users on iOS 17+.
The expected number of users. If this value is greater than the value for the maximum possible number of users that the device supports, the MDM server uses that value instead.

The timeout, in seconds, for the user session. The user session logs out automatically after the specified period of inactivity. The minimum value is 30 seconds. Setting this value to `0` removes the timeout.

Available in iOS 14.5 and later.

The timeout, in seconds, for the temporary session. The temporary session logs out automatically after the specified period of inactivity. The minimum value is 30 seconds. Setting this value to `0` removes the timeout.

Available in iOS 14.5 and later.

If `true`, the user only sees the Guest Welcome pane and can only log in as a guest user.

If `false`, the user can sign in with a Managed Apple Account (the existing behavior).

Available in iOS 14.5 and later.

A list of domains that the Shared iPad login screen displays. The user can pick a domain from the list to complete their Managed Apple Account.

If this list contains more than 3 domains, the system picks 3 at random for display. Available in iOS 16 and later.

However, the system requires an online authentication (against Apple's identity server) after the number of days specified by this setting.

Setting this value to 0 enforces online authentication every time.

items.title: Available OS Updates Command
items.summary: Get a list of available operating-system updates for a device.
items.searchKeys: AvailableOSUpdates AvailableOSUpdatesItem SupplementalOSVersionExtra SupplementalBuildVersion IsSecurityResponse RequiresBootstrapToken DeferredUntil AllowsInstallLater RestartRequired IsMajorOSUpdate IsFirmwareUpdate IsConfigDataUpdate IsCritical AppIdentifiersToClose AppIdentifiersToCloseItem InstallSize DownloadSize Build Version ProductName MetadataURL HumanReadableNameLocale HumanReadableName ProductKey
items.searchContent:
Get a list of available operating-system updates for a device. A device must have a total of `DownloadSize` + `InstallSize` bytes available to successfully install a software update. In macOS, execute the `ScheduleOSUpdateScan` command to update the results that this command returns. In iOS and tvOS, the list only contains the latest available updates.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of dictionaries that contains only the most recent available updates in iOS and tvOS, and possibly multiple available updates in macOS. Follow the instructions in the Managed Apps and Updates section of the Apple Software Lookup Service to find a complete catalog of iOS and tvOS updates.

In macOS 14 and later, `AvailableOSUpdates` doesn't include InstallAssistant-based, full-replacement installers. It only contains over-the-air (OTA) updates. OTA updates can update or upgrade the OS and support all `InstallAction` options.

If a Software Update is actively managed by a Declarative Device Management Specific Enforcement configuration, the device ignores this command as it applies to the actively managed update. This command can return information for unmanaged updates, such as System Applications and Configuration Data. For information about available updates when using Declarative Device Management, see [Using the Apple Software Lookup Service](https://support.apple.com/guide/deployment/depafd2fad80/web). The response dictionary that describes the available operating-system updates item. The Background Security Improvement OS version suffix, for example, `(a)`. Only present if this is a Background Security Improvement update. The build version for the Background Security Improvement update, for example, `13A999`, which is the same as `Build`. If `true`, this update is a Background Security Improvement. If `true`, the device can accept a Bootstrap Token from the MDM server instead of prompting for user authentication prior to installation. This only applies when `BootstrapTokenAllowedForAuthentication` is `true` in the `SecurityInfo` response. This value is available for a Mac with Apple silicon in macOS 11 and later. If present, the date when you want the update to install. This value is available in macOS 10.12.4 and later. If `true`, download the software update and install it later. If `true`, the device restarts after installing the update. If `true`, this is a major update; for example, 10.15.x to 11. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. If `true`, this is an update to firmware. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. If `true`, this is an update to a configuration file. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. If `true`, this is a critical update. An array that contains app identifiers of apps to close so you can install the update. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. The storage size necessary to install the update. This value is available in iOS 9.0 and later, and tvOS 12.0 and later. The storage size necessary to download the software update. Prior to macOS 10.14, this only includes major operating-system updates. In macOS 10.14 and later, this also includes minor updates. The build number of the update. The version of the update. The product name; for example, _iOS_. This value is available in iOS 9.0 and later, and tvOS 12.0 and later. A URL where the MDM server can request additional localized names for this update. This key isn't present for certain updates, such as mobile software updates (MSUs) or major OS updates. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. The locale, in IOS639-1 Alpha-2 code format, of the `HumanReadableName` value. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later. The human-readable name of the update in the current user's current locale. The product key that represents the update. Queries the device for a list of available OS updates. On OS X, a ScheduleOSUpdateScan must be performed to update the results returned by this query.

items.title: Schedule OS Update Command
items.summary: Schedule an update of the operating system on a device.
items.searchKeys: UpdateResults UpdateResultsItem ErrorChain ErrorChainItem ANY Status InstallAction ProductKey Updates UpdatesItem Priority MaxUserDeferrals InstallAction ProductVersion ProductKey
items.searchContent:
Schedule an update of the operating system on a device. Only supervised iOS, macOS, and tvOS devices are eligible for software update management.

This command can only schedule operating-system updates in iOS and tvOS, however, it can also schedule a variety of system software updates in macOS.

Downloading and installing updates in iOS and tvOS is a two-step process. Send a `ScheduleOSUpdate` command with `Default` for `InstallAction` to download the updates. Then send another `ScheduleOSUpdate` command with a `Default` `InstallAction` to install the updates. Software updates may require a restart, which prevents the device from responding. When this happens, the MDM server resends the `ScheduleOSUpdate` command when the device checks in again, however, the device won't return a value for `UpdateResults`.

This command uses the `UpdatesItem` `InstallAction` values to offer varying degrees of control to the user of a device. The user can control the update with the `NotifyOnly` and `DownloadOnly` actions, which don't initiate the update process at all. The `InstallASAP` and `InstallForceRestart` actions attempt to install the update as soon as possible. On iOS devices with a passcode, the user must authorize the update by entering their passcode, allowing them to defer the update a limited number of times. After the user reaches that limit, the system prompts to update every time the device returns to the Home Screen. This makes the device virtually unusable until the user approves the software update. On macOS devices, the `InstallLater` action provides a similar behavior, which specifies how many times the user may defer the update before it's forced.

A device may return a different `InstallAction` than requested.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of dictionaries that describes the results of processing operating-system updates. The response dictionary that describes the result of processing an operating-system update. A dictionary that describes an error chain. A dictionary that describes an error chain item. The error details. The status of the update, which is one of the following values:

- `Idle`: The update is idle.
- `Downloading`: The software update is downloading.
- `DownloadFailed`: The download failed.
- `DownloadRequiresComputer`: Tether the device to download this update. This value is only available in iOS.
- `DownloadInsufficientSpace`: There isn't enough space to download the update.
- `DownloadInsufficientPower`: There isn't enough power to download the update.
- `DownloadInsufficientNetwork`: The network capacity is insufficient to download the update.
- `Installing`: The software update is installing.
- `InstallInsufficientSpace`: There isn't enough space to install the update.
- `InstallInsufficientPower`: There isn't enough power to install the update.
- `InstallPhoneCallInProgress`: Installation couldn't occur because a phone call is in progress.
- `InstallFailed`: Installation failed due to an unspecified reason. The install action that the device scheduled, which is one of the following values:

- `Error`: An error occurred during scheduling.
- `DownloadOnly`: Download the software update without installing it.
- `InstallASAP`: Install a previously downloaded software update.
- `NotifyOnly`: Download the software update and notify the user through the App Store. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallLater`: Download the software update and install it at a later time. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallForceRestart`: Perform the `Default` action, and then force a restart if the update requires it. This value is available in macOS 11 and later. The product key that represents the update. An array of dictionaries specifying the updates to download or install. If this value is missing, the device applies the default behavior for handling updates.
The device ignores this command and an informational error is returned, if a software update is managed by a Declarative Device Management `SoftwareUpdateEnforcementSpecific` configuration, as the configuration takes precedence. A dictionary that describes the available operating-system updates item. The scheduling priority for downloading and preparing the requested update. This is only supported for minor OS updates (macOS 12.x to 12.y).

Available in macOS 12.3 and later. Prior versions of macOS used a priority of `Low`. The maximum number of times the system allows the user to postpone an update before it's installed. The system prompts the user once a day.

This key is only supported when `InstallAction` is `InstallLater` and only supported for minor OS updates (for example, macOS 12.x to 12.y). The install action, which is one of the following values:

- `Default`: Download or install the update, depending on the current state. You can check the `UpdateResults` dictionary to review scheduled updates. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 10.11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `DownloadOnly`: Download the software update without installing it. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `InstallASAP`: In iOS and tvOS, install a previously downloaded software update. In macOS, download the software update and trigger the restart countdown notification. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 10.11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `NotifyOnly`: Download the software update and notify the user through the App Store. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallLater`: Download the software update and install it at a later time. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallForceRestart`: Perform the `Default` action, and then force a restart if the update requires it. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.


> Warning:
> `InstallForceRestart` may result in data loss. The version of the update, which the system requires if `ProductKey` isn't present. This value is available in iOS 11.3 and later, macOS 12 and later, and tvOS 12.2 and later.

> Note:
> This value isn't available for use with Background Security Improvement updates. The product key that represents the update. This command allows the server to schedule an OS update.

An array of dictionaries specifying the updates to download or install. If this value is missing, the device applies the default behavior for handling updates.

The version of the update, which the system requires if `ProductKey` isn't present. This value is available in iOS 11.3 and later, macOS 12 and later, and tvOS 12.2 and later.

> Note:
> This value isn't available for use with Background Security Improvement updates.

The install action, which is one of the following values:

- `Default`: Download or install the update, depending on the current state. You can check the `UpdateResults` dictionary to review scheduled updates. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 10.11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `DownloadOnly`: Download the software update without installing it. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `InstallASAP`: In iOS and tvOS, install a previously downloaded software update. In macOS, download the software update and trigger the restart countdown notification. This value is available in iOS 9 and later, macOS 10.11 and later, and tvOS 12 and later.
- `NotifyOnly`: Download the software update and notify the user through the App Store. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallLater`: Download the software update and install it at a later time. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallForceRestart`: Perform the `Default` action, and then force a restart if the update requires it. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.


> Warning:
> `InstallForceRestart` may result in data loss.

The maximum number of times the system allows the user to postpone an update before it's installed. The system prompts the user once a day.

This key is only supported when `InstallAction` is `InstallLater` and only supported for minor OS updates (for example, macOS 12.x to 12.y).

The scheduling priority for downloading and preparing the requested update. This is only supported for minor OS updates (macOS 12.x to 12.y).

Available in macOS 12.3 and later. Prior versions of macOS used a priority of `Low`.

responsekeys.content: An array of dictionaries that describes the results of processing operating-system updates.

subkeys.content: The response dictionary that describes the result of processing an operating-system update.
The product key that represents the update.

The install action that the device scheduled, which is one of the following values:

- `Error`: An error occurred during scheduling.
- `DownloadOnly`: Download the software update without installing it.
- `InstallASAP`: Install a previously downloaded software update.
- `NotifyOnly`: Download the software update and notify the user through the App Store. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallLater`: Download the software update and install it at a later time. This value is available in macOS 10.11 and later.
- `InstallForceRestart`: Perform the `Default` action, and then force a restart if the update requires it. This value is available in macOS 11 and later.

The status of the update, which is one of the following values:

- `Idle`: The update is idle.
- `Downloading`: The software update is downloading.
- `DownloadFailed`: The download failed.
- `DownloadRequiresComputer`: Tether the device to download this update. This value is only available in iOS.
- `DownloadInsufficientSpace`: There isn't enough space to download the update.
- `DownloadInsufficientPower`: There isn't enough power to download the update.
- `DownloadInsufficientNetwork`: The network capacity is insufficient to download the update.
- `Installing`: The software update is installing.
- `InstallInsufficientSpace`: There isn't enough space to install the update.
- `InstallInsufficientPower`: There isn't enough power to install the update.
- `InstallPhoneCallInProgress`: Installation couldn't occur because a phone call is in progress.
- `InstallFailed`: Installation failed due to an unspecified reason.
A dictionary that describes an error chain.
A dictionary that describes an error chain item.
The error details.

notes.content:
Only supervised iOS, macOS, and tvOS devices are eligible for software update management.

This command can only schedule operating-system updates in iOS and tvOS, however, it can also schedule a variety of system software updates in macOS.

Downloading and installing updates in iOS and tvOS is a two-step process. Send a `ScheduleOSUpdate` command with `Default` for `InstallAction` to download the updates. Then send another `ScheduleOSUpdate` command with a `Default` `InstallAction` to install the updates. Software updates may require a restart, which prevents the device from responding. When this happens, the MDM server resends the `ScheduleOSUpdate` command when the device checks in again, however, the device won't return a value for `UpdateResults`.

This command uses the `UpdatesItem` `InstallAction` values to offer varying degrees of control to the user of a device. The user can control the update with the `NotifyOnly` and `DownloadOnly` actions, which don't initiate the update process at all. The `InstallASAP` and `InstallForceRestart` actions attempt to install the update as soon as possible. On iOS devices with a passcode, the user must authorize the update by entering their passcode, allowing them to defer the update a limited number of times. After the user reaches that limit, the system prompts to update every time the device returns to the Home Screen. This makes the device virtually unusable until the user approves the software update. On macOS devices, the `InstallLater` action provides a similar behavior, which specifies how many times the user may defer the update before it's forced.

A device may return a different `InstallAction` than requested.

items.searchContent:
Get the status of operating-system updates on a device. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. An array of dictionaries that describes the statuses of software updates. The array is empty if there are no software updates currently in progress.
This command only returns the status for System Applications and Configuration Data updates when a software update is managed by a Declarative Device Management `SoftwareUpdateEnforcementSpecific` configuration. A dictionary that describes the status of a software update. The dates/times when the OS notified the user about installing this OS update.

Available in macOS 12.3 and later. The date of the next attempt at installing this OS update.

Available in macOS 12.3 and later. The number of remaining user deferrals for this OS update.

Available in macOS 12.3 and later. The number of times a user can defer this OS update.

- `Idle`: The update is idle.
- `Downloading`: The software update is downloading and subsequently preparing.
- `Installing`: The software update is installing. A floating-point number between `0.0` and `1.0` that indicates the download progress as a percentage. If `true`, the update has finished downloading. The product key that represents the update. Queries the device for the status of software updates.

items.title: Delete User Command
items.summary: Delete a user's account from a device.
items.searchKeys: DeleteAllUsers ForceDeletion UserName
items.searchContent:
Delete a user's account from a device. An error response uses one of the following error codes:

- `12071`: The user doesn't exist.
- `12072`: The user is currently logged in.
- `12073`: The user has data to sync and ForceDeletion is false or unspecified.
- `12074`: Unable to delete the user. In macOS, this error code also returns for an attempt to delete the last administrator account. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. If `true`, the system attempts to delete all users from the device. If `ForceDeletion` is `false`, the system generates an error instead and doesn't delete users who have data that's pending sync. This value is available in iOS 14 and later. If `true`, the system deletes the account even if the user has data that's pending sync to the cloud. This value is available on iOS 9.3 and later. The user name of the account to delete. This key is required when the value for `DeleteAllUsers` is absent or `false`. This command allows the server to delete a user that has an active account on the device.

declaration.title: Delete User Command
declaration.description: Delete a user's account from a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to delete a user that has an active account on the device.

payloadkeys.content: The user name of the account to delete. This key is required when the value for `DeleteAllUsers` is absent or `false`.
If `true`, the system deletes the account even if the user has data that's pending sync to the cloud. This value is available on iOS 9.3 and later.

If `true`, the system attempts to delete all users from the device. If `ForceDeletion` is `false`, the system generates an error instead and doesn't delete users who have data that's pending sync. This value is available in iOS 14 and later.

- `12074`: Unable to delete the user. In macOS, this error code also returns for an attempt to delete the last administrator account.

items.summary: Force the current user to log out of a device.
items.searchContent:
Force the current user to log out of a device. After logging out the user, MDM commands aren't available on the device for up to 2 minutes.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. This command allows the server to force the current user to logout.

declaration.title: Log Out User Command
declaration.description: Force the current user to log out of a device.

payload.content: This command allows the server to force the current user to logout.

notes.content:
After logging out the user, MDM commands aren't available on the device for up to 2 minutes.

Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Unlock User Account Command
items.summary: Unlock a user account that the system locked because of too many failed password attempts.
items.searchKeys: UserName
items.searchContent: Unlock a user account that the system locked because of too many failed password attempts. Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response. The user name of the local account, which can be any local account on the system, not just a managed user account. This command allows the server to unlock a local user account that has been locked due to too many failed password attempts. Requires "Device lock and passcode removal right".

declaration.title: Unlock User Account Command
declaration.description: Unlock a user account that the system locked because of too many failed password attempts.

payload.content: This command allows the server to unlock a local user account that has been locked due to too many failed password attempts. Requires "Device lock and passcode removal right".

payloadkeys.content: The user name of the local account, which can be any local account on the system, not just a managed user account.

notes.content: Refer to the following sections to determine supported channels and requirements, and to see an example request and response.

items.title: Error Code Platform SSO Required
items.summary: An error response that indicates Platform SSO is required.
items.searchKeys: details AuthURL Package PinningRevocationCheckRequired PinningCerts PinningCertsItem ManifestURL ProfileURL message description code
items.searchContent:
An error response that indicates Platform SSO is required. The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The MDM server returns this response when a device enrolls in MDM during Setup Assistant and it requires the user to sign-in using Platform SSO before it allows enrollment and setup to proceed. A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code. The URL the device uses to create an `ASWebAuthenticationSession` to trigger Platform SSO authentication, once the profile and app are installed. A dictionary that specifies the package that the device uses to install an app with the SSO app extension used for Platform SSO. If `true`, certificate revocation checks require a positive response when using certificate pinning with `PinningCerts`. An array of DER-encoded certificates to pin the connection when fetching the `ManifestURL`. A certificate in DER-encoded format. The URL of the app manifest, which needs to begin with `https:`. The URL of the profile containing an `ExtensibleSingleSignOn` profile payload that the device uses to configure the SSO extension for Platform SSO. A description of the error to display to the user. A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user. Indicates that the device needs to do Platform SSO before enrollment and setup can proceed.

declaration.title: Error Code Platform SSO Required
declaration.description: An error response that indicates Platform SSO is required.

payloadkeys.content: Indicates that the device needs to do Platform SSO before enrollment and setup can proceed.
A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user.
A description of the error to display to the user.
A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code.

subkeys.content: The URL of the profile containing an `ExtensibleSingleSignOn` profile payload that the device uses to configure the SSO extension for Platform SSO.
A dictionary that specifies the package that the device uses to install an app with the SSO app extension used for Platform SSO.
The URL of the app manifest, which needs to begin with `https:`.
An array of DER-encoded certificates to pin the connection when fetching the `ManifestURL`.
A certificate in DER-encoded format.
If `true`, certificate revocation checks require a positive response when using certificate pinning with `PinningCerts`.
The URL the device uses to create an `ASWebAuthenticationSession` to trigger Platform SSO authentication, once the profile and app are installed.

items.title: Error Code Software Update Required
items.summary: An error response that indicates the system requires a software update.
items.searchKeys: details RequireBetaProgram Token Description BuildVersion OSVersion message description code
items.searchContent:
An error response that indicates the system requires a software update. The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The MDM server returns this response when a device enrolls in MDM during Setup Assistant and it requires the device to perform a software update before it can continue with enrollment and setup. A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code. The device enrolls in the beta program, allowing enforced software updates to beta program OS versions. The device remains in the beta program after the system completes the enforced software update. The AxM seeding service token for the AxM organization the MDM server is part of. The system uses this token to enroll the device in the corresponding beta program. A human readable description of the beta program. The build version that the device needs to update to, for example, "20A242. The systems uses the build version for testing during seeding periods. This identifier can include a supplemental version identifier, for example, "20A242a". If the `BuildVersion` isn't consistent with the `OSVersion`, `OSVersion` take precedence. The OS version that the device needs to update to, for example, "16.1". This identifier can include a supplemental version identifier, for example, "16.1 (a)". A description of the error to display to the user. A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user. Indicates that the device needs to perform a software update before enrollment and setup can proceed.

declaration.description: An error response that indicates the system requires a software update.

A description of the error to display to the user.
A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code.

subkeys.content: The OS version that the device needs to update to, for example, "16.1". This identifier can include a supplemental version identifier, for example, "16.1 (a)".

The build version that the device needs to update to, for example, "20A242. The systems uses the build version for testing during seeding periods. This identifier can include a supplemental version identifier, for example, "20A242a". If the `BuildVersion` isn't consistent with the `OSVersion`, `OSVersion` take precedence.
The device enrolls in the beta program, allowing enforced software updates to beta program OS versions. The device remains in the beta program after the system completes the enforced software update.
A human readable description of the beta program.
The AxM seeding service token for the AxM organization the MDM server is part of. The system uses this token to enroll the device in the corresponding beta program.

items.title: Error Unrecognized Device
items.summary: An error response that indicates a device needs to unenroll.
items.searchKeys: message description code
items.searchContent:
An error response that indicates a device needs to unenroll. The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The MDM server returns this response when it doesn't recognize the device making the request. This causes the device to unenroll from the MDM server. Use this error instead of the server returning a 401 response to cause an unenroll. A description of the error to display to the user. A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user. Indicates that the device is not recognized by the server. This causes the device to unenroll from MDM.

declaration.description: An error response that indicates a device needs to unenroll.

The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The MDM server returns this response when it doesn't recognize the device making the request. This causes the device to unenroll from the MDM server. Use this error instead of the server returning a 401 response to cause an unenroll.

items.title: Error Code Pairing Token Missing
items.summary: An error response that indicates a missing pairing token.
items.searchKeys: details security-token message description code
items.searchContent:
An error response that indicates a missing pairing token. The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The system returns this response when an Apple Watch enrolls in MDM, but the watch doesn't include a `PAIRING_TOKEN` in the `MachineInfo` request. After the watch receives this response, it fetches a pairing token from the phone's MDM server through a request to the phone. Then, the watch repeats the enrollment request and includes the pairing token. A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code. The security token to pass to the phone's MDM server to create the pairing token. This token needs to be a random UUID string. A description of the error to display to the user. A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user. Indicates that the pairing token, which the system requires to enroll the watch, is missing.

declaration.title: Error Code Pairing Token Missing
declaration.description: An error response that indicates a missing pairing token.

payloadkeys.content: Indicates that the pairing token, which the system requires to enroll the watch, is missing.
A description of the error. Only use this for logging purposes and don't display it to the user.
A description of the error to display to the user.
A dictionary that contains additional data about the error code.

subkeys.content: The security token to pass to the phone's MDM server to create the pairing token. This token needs to be a random UUID string.

notes.content:
The schema for a JSON or property list XML document that an MDM server's 403 response body contains. The response headers need to include a "Content-Type" header that indicates whether the response returns JSON or XML.

The system returns this response when an Apple Watch enrolls in MDM, but the watch doesn't include a `PAIRING_TOKEN` in the `MachineInfo` request. After the watch receives this response, it fetches a pairing token from the phone's MDM server through a request to the phone. Then, the watch repeats the enrollment request and includes the pairing token.

items.title: Common Payload Keys
items.summary: The properties common to all payloads.
items.searchKeys: PayloadOrganization PayloadDisplayName PayloadDescription PayloadVersion PayloadType PayloadUUID PayloadIdentifier
items.searchContent:
The properties common to all payloads. The human-readable string containing the name of the organization that provides the profile. This value doesn't need to match the organization payload value in the enclosing dictionary. The human-readable name for the profile payload. The name appears on the Detail screen and doesn't need to be unique. The human-readable description of this payload. This description appears on the Detail screen. The version of this specific payload. The payload type, which each payload domain's reference page specifies. The globally unique identifier for the payload. The actual content is unimportant, but must be globally unique. In macOS, use `uuidgen` to generate UUIDs.

During a profile replacement, the system updates payloads with the same `PayloadIdentifier` and `PayloadUUID` in the old and new profiles. The reverse-DNS-style identifier for the payload. This identifier is usually the same as the `TopLevel` value, with an additional appended component. This string must be unique within the profile.

During a profile replacement, the system updates payloads with the same `PayloadIdentifier` and `PayloadUUID` in the old and new profiles.

The reverse-DNS-style identifier for the payload. This identifier is usually the same as the `TopLevel` value, with an additional appended component. This string must be unique within the profile.

During a profile replacement, the system updates payloads with the same `PayloadIdentifier` and `PayloadUUID` in the old and new profiles.

The globally unique identifier for the payload. The actual content is unimportant, but must be globally unique. In macOS, use `uuidgen` to generate UUIDs.

During a profile replacement, the system updates payloads with the same `PayloadIdentifier` and `PayloadUUID` in the old and new profiles.
The payload type, which each payload domain's reference page specifies.
The version of this specific payload.

items.title: Top Level
items.summary: The top-level payload properties for all profiles.
items.searchKeys: ConsentText ConsentTextItem ANY TargetDeviceType PayloadExpirationDate DurationUntilRemoval RemovalDate PayloadScope PayloadRemovalDisallowed PayloadOrganization PayloadDisplayName PayloadDescription EncryptedPayloadContent PayloadContent PayloadContentItem ANY PayloadVersion PayloadType PayloadUUID PayloadIdentifier
items.searchContent:
The top-level payload properties for all profiles. A dictionary that includes:

- A key that contains the IETF BCP 47 identifier for a language, such as _en_ or _jp_
- A value that contains the agreement localized to language specified by the key

The dictionary can also contain an optional key, `default`, with its value consisting of the unlocalized (usually in _en_) agreement.

The system always displays the agreement in a dialog, and the user needs to agree before the system can install the profile.

The system chooses a localized version in the order of preference that the user specifies in macOS, or based on the user's current language setting in iOS. If there's no exact match, the system uses the default localization. If there's no default localization, the system uses the _en_ localization. If there's no _en_ localization, the system uses the first available localization.

> Tip:
> Provide a default value, if possible. The system won't display a warning if the user's locale doesn't match any localization in the `ConsentText` dictionary. The dictionary containing a key that consists of the IETF BCP 47 identifier for a language (for example, en or jp) and a value that consists of the agreement localized to that language. The key consisting of the IETF BCP 47 identifier for a language (for example, en or jp) and the value consisting of the agreement localized to that language. The type of platform of the target device. Specifying the platform type helps prevent unintended installations.

For interactive installations on iOS devices, specifying a target platform avoids interstitial alerts that prompt the user to choose a profile target when multiple targets are eligible.

Allowed values:

- `0`: Any/unspecified
- `1`: iPhone/iPad/iPod Touch
- `2`: Apple Watch
- `3`: HomePod
- `4`: Apple TV
- `5`: Mac
- `6`: Vision Pro The date when a profile is no longer valid and the system presents an update button to the user. The number of seconds until the profile is automatically removed. If the `RemovalDate` key is present, the system uses whichever field yields the earliest date. The date when the system automatically removes the profile. A string that defines whether to install the profile for the system or the user. In many cases, it determines the location of certificate items, such as keychains. Though it's not possible to declare different payload scopes, payloads like VPN can automatically install their items in both scopes, if needed. If present and set to `true`, the user can't delete the profile unless the profile has a removal password and the user provides it.

On macOS 10.15 and later, this key only affects removal of _manually_ installed profiles. If set to `true` and no profile removal payload is present, removing the profile requires admin auth.

On macOS versions prior to 10.15, this key prevents admins from removing MDM installed profiles. However, as of macOS 10.15, users can never remove MDM profiles, not even the admin.

On iOS users can't remove a MDM profile.

Requires a supervised device. The human-readable string that contains the name of the organization that provided the profile. The human-readable name for the profile, which doesn't need to be unique. The system displays this value on the Detail screen. The description of the profile, shown on the Detail screen for the profile. Make this description detailed enough to help the user decide whether to install the profile. Enabled if `IsEncrypted` is `true`. The array of payload dictionaries. If `IsEncrypted` is `true`, this array isn't needed. The payload-specific content for this profile. A payload item as defined by each payload type. The version number of the profile format, which needs to be `1`. This number represents the version of the configuration profile as a whole, not of the individual profiles within it. The type of payload. The only supported value is `Configuration`. The globally unique identifier for the profile. The actual content is unimportant. In macOS, you can use `uuidgen` to generate reasonable UUIDs. The reverse-DNS style identifier (`com.example.myprofile`, for example) that identifies the profile. The system uses this string to determine whether to replace an existing profile or add it as a new profile.

items.title: Active Directory Certificate
items.summary: The payload that configures Active Directory Certificate settings.
items.searchKeys: EnableAutoRenewal Keysize KeyIsExtractable PromptForCredentials AllowAllAppsAccess CertificateAcquisitionMechanism CertificateAuthority CertificateRenewalTimeInterval Description CertTemplate CertServer
items.searchTitles: Active Directory Certificate  Enable Auto Renewal Key Size Key Is Extractable Prompt for Credentials Allow All Apps Access Certificate Acquisition Mechanism Certificate Authority Certificate Renewal Time Interval Description Certificate Template Certificate Server
items.searchContent:
The payload that configures Active Directory Certificate settings. To get a certificate from a Microsoft CA, follow the instructions at [Request a certificate from a Microsoft Certificate Authority](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204602). If `true`, the certificate obtained with this payload attempts auto-renewal. Auto-renewal can only be used with device Active Directory certificate payloads. Available in macOS 10.13.4 and later. The RSA key size for the certificate signing request (CSR). Available in macOS 10.11 and later. If `true`, the system allows exporting the private key. Available in macOS 10.10 and later. If `true`, the system prompts the user for credentials when is installs the profile. This key applies only to user certificates with the Manual Download profile delivery method. Omit this key for computer certificates. Available in macOS 10.8 and later. If `true`, gives apps access to the private key. Available in macOS 10.10 and later. This value is most commonly `RPC`; if using web enrollment, use `HTTP`. Available in macOS 10.8 and later. The name of the certificate authority (CA), which is determined from the common name (CN) of the Active Directory entry. Available in macOS 10.8 and later. Valid values:

- CN=<your CA Name>
- CN=`Certification Authorities`
- CN=`Public Key Services`
- CN=`Services`
- CN=`Configuration`
- CN=<your base Domain Name> The number of days in advance of certificate expiration that the notification center notifies the user. A user-friendly description of the certification identity. The certificate template for your environment. The default user certificate value is \`User\`. The default computer certificate value is \`Machine\`. The fully qualified host name of the CA. A certificate can be requested from a Microsoft Certificate Authority (CA) using DCE/RPC and the Active Directory Certificate profile payload instructions detailed at support.apple.com/kb/HT5357.

declaration.title: Active Directory Certificate

payloadkeys.content: The fully qualified host name of the CA.

payloadkeys.title: Certificate Template
payloadkeys.content: The certificate template for your environment. The default user certificate value is \`User\`. The default computer certificate value is \`Machine\`.

payloadkeys.title: Description
payloadkeys.content: A user-friendly description of the certification identity.

payloadkeys.title: Certificate Renewal Time Interval
payloadkeys.content: The number of days in advance of certificate expiration that the notification center notifies the user.

payloadkeys.title: Certificate Authority
payloadkeys.content:
The name of the certificate authority (CA), which is determined from the common name (CN) of the Active Directory entry. Available in macOS 10.8 and later. Valid values:

- CN=<your CA Name>
- CN=`Certification Authorities`
- CN=`Public Key Services`
- CN=`Services`
- CN=`Configuration`
- CN=<your base Domain Name>

payloadkeys.title: Certificate Acquisition Mechanism
payloadkeys.content: This value is most commonly `RPC`; if using web enrollment, use `HTTP`. Available in macOS 10.8 and later.

payloadkeys.title: Allow All Apps Access
payloadkeys.content: If `true`, gives apps access to the private key. Available in macOS 10.10 and later.

payloadkeys.title: Prompt for Credentials
payloadkeys.content: If `true`, the system prompts the user for credentials when is installs the profile. This key applies only to user certificates with the Manual Download profile delivery method. Omit this key for computer certificates. Available in macOS 10.8 and later.

payloadkeys.title: Key Is Extractable
payloadkeys.content: If `true`, the system allows exporting the private key. Available in macOS 10.10 and later.

payloadkeys.title: Key Size
payloadkeys.content: The RSA key size for the certificate signing request (CSR). Available in macOS 10.11 and later.

payloadkeys.title: Enable Auto Renewal
payloadkeys.content: If `true`, the certificate obtained with this payload attempts auto-renewal. Auto-renewal can only be used with device Active Directory certificate payloads. Available in macOS 10.13.4 and later.

notes.content: To get a certificate from a Microsoft CA, follow the instructions at [Request a certificate from a Microsoft Certificate Authority](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204602).
